changes through response, parody, quotation, or novelistic orchestration. perspective is
voice-position, temporality is chronotope as time-space of narrative, schema or paradigm
marks speech genres, and records evaluative accent. This is the hermeneutic sheaf theorem: polyphonic meaning is the
gluing of voice-local horizons without reducing them to a single monologic authority. For
this reason the chapter should be heard through the hermeneutic sheaf theorem. The hermeneutic sheaf theorem is the hermeneutic sheaf theorem,
where local meanings glue into a global one when they agree on overlaps. The indexical pullback theorem is the
indexical pullback theorem, where preservation and emergence fail to translate cleanly. The profundity theorem
is the profundity theorem, where depth comes from breadth across horizons and closure under
emergence.

## Derived primitives

The derived primitives make the diagnosis compact. perspective: Dialogism makes every
utterance perspectival, oriented toward another voice's possible response. differentiation:
Heteroglossia differentiates social languages, genres, and accents within the novelistic
field. temporality: The chronotope binds temporal and spatial horizons into genre-specific
narrative intelligibility. R. Polyphony lets consciousness appear reflexively through its
relation to other independent voices. None of these is introduced as an extra metaphysical
ingredient. Each is derived from the ten-tuple: intentionality from resonance across
horizons, temporality from ordered horizon-translation, perspective from a home region in
the horizon category, differentiation from suppression, and reflexion from self-application
of the horizon functor.

## Chapter summary

Mikhail Bakhtin therefore enters idea theory as a disciplined local algebra of meaning. The
chapter has followed the promised path: biography, position, algebraic re-statement, theorem
re-statement, addition, divergence, horizon structure, and derived primitives. The result is
not a replacement for the thinker, but a listening guide to the formal pressure already
present in the work.


# Chapter 65 — Charles Sanders Peirce

This chapter treats Charles Sanders Peirce as a test case for idea theory. We keep the
operating definitions audible: composition puts ideas together, the identity idea leaves a
composition unchanged, the resonance pairing measures directed evocation, the preservation
part, suppression part, and emergence part describe what is kept, cancelled, and newly
produced, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi —
lets the whole algebra vary by context.

## Biographical preamble

Charles Sanders Peirce, pronounced approximately *purse* lived from 1839 to 1914, worked in
the United States, and belongs here to pragmatism, logic, and semiotics. In one compressed
sentence, he said that meaning is found in conceivable practical effects. That sentence
gives the chapter its entry point: meaning is not a bare object sitting before a spectator,
but a structured achievement within a field of acts, signs, practices, or horizons.

## Position

The position can be put simply. Peirce founds pragmatism (later "pragmaticism," to
distinguish it from James's looser usage) with the maxim: to consider what effects, that
might conceivably have practical bearings, we conceive the object of our conception to have
— then our conception of these effects is the whole of our conception of the object. Meaning
is exhibited in conceivable practical consequences. The categories of Firstness, Secondness,
and Thirdness organize all phenomena. Firstness is qualitative immediacy (a felt quality),
Secondness is brute reaction (the resistance of one thing to another), Thirdness is
mediation (law, habit, sign-relation). Every phenomenon involves all three categories in
different proportions. The point is not merely historical. It tells us what kind of item is
being counted as an idea: not a loose mental image, but a content whose sense depends on the
way it is given, used, interpreted, or carried forward.

## Algebraic re-statement

In the algebraic re-statement, the basic domain is the field of contents named by the zoo
entry. Take the idea domain to be the set of signs in their full triadic structure: items as
standing for objects to interpretants. Each element is tagged by its sign-type (icon, index,
symbol), by its object-relation, and by the chain of interpretants it generates. The
identity idea is the bare sign-position — the empty triadic placeholder awaiting
determination. Composition is sign-combination: the composition of signs into more complex
signs, with the interpretant of one becoming a sign for the next. composition is constrained
by the categorial structure (Firstness, Secondness, Thirdness) and by the conventions and
habits operative in the community. The resonance pairing is interpretive proximity: how
strongly two signs participate in a common chain of interpretants and so converge on common
practical consequences. Read aloud, this says that the thinker supplies a local model of the
ten-tuple: a domain of contents, a composition rule, an identity idea, directed resonance,
the threefold division into preservation, suppression, and emergence, an emergence cocycle,
a degree of articulation, and a context-sensitive horizon structure.

## Theorem re-statement

*The theorem-style claim is this: The first theorem-shaped claim is that signification is irreducibly triadic: composition cannot be factored into dyadic operations.*

In paragraph form, the claim rules out the fantasy that meaning is already complete before
context enters. The first theorem-shaped claim is that signification is irreducibly triadic:
composition cannot be factored into dyadic operations. The Peircean insistence on triadicity
is a structural fact about the algebra. This is a the composition theorem claim with constitutive triadicity. The
second claim is the pragmatic maxim: meaning is exhausted by conceivable practical bearings.
The prediction is that failures of understanding will not be accidental noise. They will
cluster where a local content cannot be carried unchanged across a change of horizon, or
where an apparent identity hides suppressed difference.

Carefully restated, the relevant idea algebra has a domain of contents, an identity idea,
associative composition, the resonance pairing, the preservation part, the suppression part,
the emergence part, the emergence cocycle, degree, the horizon category, and the horizon
functor. The central claim says that the philosopher's privileged object is stable only when
these roles cooperate. Where local data can be glued, the chapter instantiates the profundity theorem; where
translation fails, the obstruction is also audible as a horizon-functor effect.

## What the formalism adds

What the formalism adds is clarification without replacement. First, the irreducible
triadicity of signs gains structural form: composition is essentially three-place, and any
attempt to reduce it to a sequence of dyadic relations distorts the structure. Second, the
pragmatic maxim becomes the operational definition of the resonance pairing: meaning-
equivalence is convergence of conceivable practical consequences. The maxim is not a
stipulation but a structural rule for identifying elements of the idea domain. Third,
abduction as emergence becomes a precise inferential operator distinct from
deduction (the emergence cocycle-derivation) and induction (statistical the emergence
cocycle-extrapolation). The Peircean recovery of inference to the best explanation is given
algebraic location. In ordinary language, the formalism lets us ask which operation does the
philosophical work. Is the key relation resonance, because one content fulfills or evokes
another? Is it suppression, because meaning appears through contrast? Is it the horizon
functor, because every local act changes when the context changes? The answer differs from
thinker to thinker, but the vocabulary stays stable.

## Where it diverges

The divergence is equally important. First, the architectonic ambition of Peirce's mature
philosophy — the attempt to articulate a complete system encompassing logic, semiotics,
metaphysics, cosmology, and theology — far exceeds what any algebraic instantiation can
capture. Peirce was a system-builder; the algebra is a fragment. Second, Peirce's
evolutionary cosmology — the universe as growing in habits, with chance and law as
historical phases — depends on substantive metaphysical commitments outside the structural
facts. Third, the regulative status of long-run convergence is a hope, not a theorem. The
algebra cannot guarantee that the emergence cocycle converges, and Peirce's insistence on
the regulative ideal is partly a confession of this. This is not a defect in the reading. It
marks the price of any formal reconstruction: the algebra shows the load-bearing structure,
while the original philosophy often also includes tone, method, ethical urgency, religious
practice, literary performance, or first-person discipline.

## Horizon structure and the horizon-functor theorem

The horizon structure names the context in which the local algebra lives. Peirce's the
horizon category is the semeiotic and communal-inquiry space of signs, objects,
interpretants, habits, and future investigation. Morphisms carry a sign to further
interpretants, extending meaning through an indefinitely open chain constrained by reality.
intentionality is triadic aboutness, temporality is inquiry's future orientation, credence
is fallibilist credence, and schema or paradigm is the community of investigators as a
connected horizon. This is the profundity theorem: truth is the trans-horizonal stability toward which inquiry
would converge under unlimited continuation of interpretant-morphisms. For this reason the
chapter should be heard through the profundity theorem. The hermeneutic sheaf theorem is the hermeneutic sheaf theorem, where local
meanings glue into a global one when they agree on overlaps. The indexical pullback theorem is the indexical pullback
theorem, where preservation and emergence fail to translate cleanly. The profundity theorem is the profundity
theorem, where depth comes from breadth across horizons and closure under emergence.

## Derived primitives

The derived primitives make the diagnosis compact. intentionality: The sign-object-
interpretant triad formalizes intentional aboutness as mediated semiosis. potentiality:
Abduction opens potential explanations, proposing hypotheses before deduction and induction
test them. credence: Fallibilism assigns corrigible credence, oriented toward the long-run
community of inquiry. R. Semiosis is reflexive and self-correcting, since interpretants
themselves become further signs. None of these is introduced as an extra metaphysical
ingredient. Each is derived from the ten-tuple: intentionality from resonance across
horizons, temporality from ordered horizon-translation, perspective from a home region in
the horizon category, differentiation from suppression, and reflexion from self-application
of the horizon functor.

## Chapter summary

Charles Sanders Peirce therefore enters idea theory as a disciplined local algebra of
meaning. The chapter has followed the promised path: biography, position, algebraic re-
statement, theorem re-statement, addition, divergence, horizon structure, and derived
primitives. The result is not a replacement for the thinker, but a listening guide to the
formal pressure already present in the work.


# Chapter 66 — William James

This chapter treats William James as a test case for idea theory. We keep the operating
definitions audible: composition puts ideas together, the identity idea leaves a composition
unchanged, the resonance pairing measures directed evocation, the preservation part,
suppression part, and emergence part describe what is kept, cancelled, and newly produced,
and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — lets the
whole algebra vary by context.

## Biographical preamble

William James, pronounced approximately *jaymz* lived from 1842 to 1910, worked in the
United States, and belongs here to pragmatism and psychology. In one compressed sentence, he
wrote that truth happens to an idea. That sentence gives the chapter its entry point:
meaning is not a bare object sitting before a spectator, but a structured achievement within
a field of acts, signs, practices, or horizons.

## Position

The position can be put simply. James's pragmatism is more practical and pluralistic than
Peirce's. The pragmatic method is to settle metaphysical disputes by tracing the practical
consequences of each position; if no practical difference can be exhibited, the dispute is
verbal. Truth is what works, what pays in the long run of experience. The Principles of
Psychology (1890) develops the stream of consciousness as the basic datum: experience as
continuous flow rather than discrete sensations. Each pulse of consciousness has a fringe —
a halo of relations and tendencies that surrounds the focal content and gives it its sense.
The point is not merely historical. It tells us what kind of item is being counted as an
idea: not a loose mental image, but a content whose sense depends on the way it is given,
used, interpreted, or carried forward.

## Algebraic re-statement

In the algebraic re-statement, the basic domain is the field of contents named by the zoo
entry. Take the idea domain to be the set of pulses of pure experience (in James's late
terminology): items as moments of conscious life, including the relations among them. Each
element is tagged by its focal content, its fringe of relations, and its position in the
stream. The identity idea is the empty present-moment — the bare felt now. Composition is
the running of the stream: the continuous transition from one pulse to the next, with the
fringe of the prior pulse becoming the focal content of the next or being held as a
tendency. composition is constrained by the laws of psychology (association, attention,
habit). The resonance pairing is felt continuity: the degree to which two pulses belong to
the same stream and share fringes. Read aloud, this says that the thinker supplies a local
model of the ten-tuple: a domain of contents, a composition rule, an identity idea, directed
resonance, the threefold division into preservation, suppression, and emergence, an
emergence cocycle, a degree of articulation, and a context-sensitive horizon structure.

## Theorem re-statement

*The theorem-style claim is this: The first theorem-shaped claim is that experience is continuous: the stream of consciousness is the basic datum, and discrete sensations are abstractions from it.*

In paragraph form, the claim rules out the fantasy that meaning is already complete before
context enters. The first theorem-shaped claim is that experience is continuous: the stream
of consciousness is the basic datum, and discrete sensations are abstractions from it.
composition is everywhere active, with no atomic gaps. This is a the composition theorem claim with strong
continuist content. The second claim is the pragmatic conception of truth: the resonance
pairing tracks practical cash-value. The prediction is that failures of understanding will
not be accidental noise. They will cluster where a local content cannot be carried unchanged
across a change of horizon, or where an apparent identity hides suppressed difference.

Carefully restated, the relevant idea algebra has a domain of contents, an identity idea,
associative composition, the resonance pairing, the preservation part, the suppression part,
the emergence part, the emergence cocycle, degree, the horizon category, and the horizon
functor. The central claim says that the philosopher's privileged object is stable only when
these roles cooperate. Where local data can be glued, the chapter instantiates the profundity theorem; where
translation fails, the obstruction is also audible as a horizon-functor effect.

## What the formalism adds

What the formalism adds is clarification without replacement. First, the stream of
consciousness becomes the structural fact that composition is everywhere continuous; the
algebra has no atomistic decomposition. The Jamesian polemic against the bricks-and-mortar
theory of mind gains algebraic backing. Second, the fringe becomes a structural feature of
every element: each relevant content carries a halo of resonance 
near elements that constitute its sense. The algebra exhibits the holism James insisted
upon. Third, the pragmatic maxim becomes the operational rule for identifying elements: two
would-be elements with identical consequences for the emergence cocycle are the same
element. In ordinary language, the formalism lets us ask which operation does the
philosophical work. Is the key relation resonance, because one content fulfills or evokes
another? Is it suppression, because meaning appears through contrast? Is it the horizon
functor, because every local act changes when the context changes? The answer differs from
thinker to thinker, but the vocabulary stays stable.

## Where it diverges

The divergence is equally important. First, James's spirited and personal prose is part of
his philosophical method; the algebra's neutral exposition loses the sympathy and the moral
seriousness that animate the Jamesian project. Second, the empirical psychology that grounds
The Principles of Psychology is a substantive scientific contribution outside the algebra.
The structural facts the formalism captures abstract from the rich empirical content. Third,
the religious sympathies that inform The Varieties of Religious Experience and The Will to
Believe involve substantive engagements with theological and mystical traditions the algebra
is silent about. Fourth, the disagreements between James and Peirce on the meaning of
pragmatism — Peirce's narrower meaning-theory versus James's broader will-to-believe — turn
on substantive philosophical commitments not settled by structural facts. This is not a
defect in the reading. It marks the price of any formal reconstruction: the algebra shows
the load-bearing structure, while the original philosophy often also includes tone, method,
ethical urgency, religious practice, literary performance, or first-person discipline.

## Horizon structure and the horizon-functor theorem

The horizon structure names the context in which the local algebra lives. James' the horizon
category is the pluralistic field of live options, experiential streams, temperaments,
practices, and consequences. Morphisms test an idea by carrying it into future experience,
where its cash-value appears in conduct and satisfaction. temporality is the stream of
consciousness, credence is practical belief under uncertainty, is the felt fringe and will-
to-believe, and schema or paradigm is plural rather than monistic world-making. This is the profundity theorem
in pragmatist form: truth is what remains stable across experiential consequences and future
verifications, not a static correspondence outside practice. For this reason the chapter
should be heard through the profundity theorem. The hermeneutic sheaf theorem is the hermeneutic sheaf theorem, where local meanings glue
into a global one when they agree on overlaps. The indexical pullback theorem is the indexical pullback theorem, where
preservation and emergence fail to translate cleanly. The profundity theorem is the profundity theorem, where
depth comes from breadth across horizons and closure under emergence.

## Derived primitives

The derived primitives make the diagnosis compact. temporality: The stream of consciousness
makes experience continuous, selective, and temporally flowing. perspective: Radical
empiricism treats relations as experienced from within concrete personal perspectives.
credence: The will to believe licenses credence where options are live, forced, and
momentous. affect or conatus: Pragmatic cash-value measures ideas by their practical
bearings and life-enhancing consequences. None of these is introduced as an extra
metaphysical ingredient. Each is derived from the ten-tuple: intentionality from resonance
across horizons, temporality from ordered horizon-translation, perspective from a home
region in the horizon category, differentiation from suppression, and reflexion from self-
application of the horizon functor.

## Chapter summary

William James therefore enters idea theory as a disciplined local algebra of meaning. The
chapter has followed the promised path: biography, position, algebraic re-statement, theorem
re-statement, addition, divergence, horizon structure, and derived primitives. The result is
not a replacement for the thinker, but a listening guide to the formal pressure already
present in the work.


# Chapter 67 — John Dewey

This chapter treats John Dewey as a test case for idea theory. We keep the operating
definitions audible: composition puts ideas together, the identity idea leaves a composition
unchanged, the resonance pairing measures directed evocation, the preservation part,
suppression part, and emergence part describe what is kept, cancelled, and newly produced,
and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — lets the
whole algebra vary by context.

## Biographical preamble

John Dewey, pronounced approximately *DOO-ee* lived from 1859 to 1952, worked in the United
States, and belongs here to pragmatism and democratic philosophy. In one compressed
sentence, he understood inquiry as transforming an indeterminate situation into a
determinate one. That sentence gives the chapter its entry point: meaning is not a bare
object sitting before a spectator, but a structured achievement within a field of acts,
signs, practices, or horizons.

## Position

The position can be put simply. Dewey's instrumentalism treats ideas as tools for solving
problems within experience. Inquiry begins in a problematic situation — a felt indeterminacy
that disrupts ongoing activity — and proceeds through the formulation of hypotheses,
experimental testing, and the achievement of a unified, determinate situation. Truth is what
inquiry yields when it succeeds. The traditional philosophical dualisms — mind versus body,
subject versus object, theory versus practice, reason versus experience — are diagnosed as
obstacles. Each pole is an abstraction from a primary unity; treating them as independent
realities generates pseudo-problems that have plagued philosophy since Descartes. The point
is not merely historical. It tells us what kind of item is being counted as an idea: not a
loose mental image, but a content whose sense depends on the way it is given, used,
interpreted, or carried forward.

## Algebraic re-statement

In the algebraic re-statement, the basic domain is the field of contents named by the zoo
entry. Take the idea domain to be the set of situations and the inquiries that traverse
them: items as integrated patterns of experience, problematic or resolved. Each element is
tagged by its degree of indeterminacy and its position in an inquiry-arc. The identity idea
is the integrated situation prior to disruption — the unproblematic ongoing activity.
Composition is inquiry: the temporal sequence by which a problematic situation is
transformed into a resolved one through hypothesis-formation, testing, and integration.
composition is constrained by the situation's indeterminacy and by the resources of the
inquirer. The resonance pairing is functional similarity: the degree to which two situations
admit similar inquiries and yield similar resolutions. Read aloud, this says that the
thinker supplies a local model of the ten-tuple: a domain of contents, a composition rule,
an identity idea, directed resonance, the threefold division into preservation, suppression,
and emergence, an emergence cocycle, a degree of articulation, and a context-sensitive
horizon structure.

## Theorem re-statement

*The theorem-style claim is this: The first theorem-shaped claim is that inquiry is the basic operation composition on situations.*

In paragraph form, the claim rules out the fantasy that meaning is already complete before
context enters. The first theorem-shaped claim is that inquiry is the basic operation
composition on situations. Knowledge is not a state but the achievement of a transformation
of situations. This is a the composition theorem claim with strong instrumentalist content. The second claim is
that ideas are tools: the resonance pairing tracks instrumental adequacy. The prediction is
that failures of understanding will not be accidental noise. They will cluster where a local
content cannot be carried unchanged across a change of horizon, or where an apparent
identity hides suppressed difference.

Carefully restated, the relevant idea algebra has a domain of contents, an identity idea,
associative composition, the resonance pairing, the preservation part, the suppression part,
the emergence part, the emergence cocycle, degree, the horizon category, and the horizon
functor. The central claim says that the philosopher's privileged object is stable only when
these roles cooperate. Where local data can be glued, the chapter instantiates the hermeneutic sheaf theorem; where
translation fails, the obstruction is also audible as a horizon-functor effect.

## What the formalism adds

What the formalism adds is clarification without replacement. First, inquiry as the central
operation gives Dewey's philosophy an algebraic shape: composition is the inquiry-
transformation, and the pattern of inquiry (problematic situation, hypothesis, test,
resolution) is the structural shape of composition-action. Second, the rejection of dualisms
becomes the structural fact that the algebra is one connected component. Apparent partitions
(mind/body, theory/practice) are not algebraic partitions but historical-philosophical
projections. Third, the instrumental conception of truth becomes the assertion that the
resonance pairing tracks fitness for inquiry-purposes rather than correspondence to a mind-
independent fact. Truth is functional, not pictorial. In ordinary language, the formalism
lets us ask which operation does the philosophical work. Is the key relation resonance,
because one content fulfills or evokes another? Is it suppression, because meaning appears
through contrast? Is it the horizon functor, because every local act changes when the
context changes? The answer differs from thinker to thinker, but the vocabulary stays
stable.

## Where it diverges

The divergence is equally important. First, the practical engagement that animates Dewey's
work — his interventions in school reform, his political activism, his sustained public
writing — is a substantive commitment the algebra cannot generate. Dewey's pragmatism is
meant to do work in the world; the formalism describes structural facts. Second, the
substantive content of "the problematic situation" depends on what the inquirer takes to be
a problem, which depends on her interests, history, and culture. The algebra does not fix
what counts as problematic; that is a contextual question. Third, the religious humanism of
A Common Faith — Dewey's late attempt to articulate a religious quality of experience
without supernaturalism — depends on substantive commitments outside structural relations.
This is not a defect in the reading. It marks the price of any formal reconstruction: the
algebra shows the load-bearing structure, while the original philosophy often also includes
tone, method, ethical urgency, religious practice, literary performance, or first-person
discipline.

## Horizon structure and the horizon-functor theorem

The horizon structure names the context in which the local algebra lives. Dewey's the
horizon category is the ecology of problematic situations, inquiry, habits, publics,
schools, and democratic institutions. Morphisms transform an indeterminate situation into a
resolved one by experimental action, not by spectatorship. temporality is inquiry's temporal
arc, potentiality is potentiality in the situation, credence is warranted assertibility, and
perspective is social intelligence distributed across a community. This is the hermeneutic sheaf theorem: knowledge
glues local operations, consequences, and revisions into a reconstructed situation whose
meaning is publicly testable. For this reason the chapter should be heard through the hermeneutic sheaf theorem. The hermeneutic sheaf theorem
is the hermeneutic sheaf theorem, where local meanings glue into a global one when they
agree on overlaps. The indexical pullback theorem is the indexical pullback theorem, where preservation and emergence
fail to translate cleanly. The profundity theorem is the profundity theorem, where depth comes from breadth
across horizons and closure under emergence.

## Derived primitives

The derived primitives make the diagnosis compact. potentiality: Inquiry begins in a
problematic situation, developing potentials into warranted resolution. temporality:
Experience is transactional and temporal, reorganizing habits through consequences over
time. schema or paradigm: Habits and democratic publics are schemas for coordinating inquiry
and social learning. credence: Warranted assertibility replaces certainty with
experimentally stabilized credence. None of these is introduced as an extra metaphysical
ingredient. Each is derived from the ten-tuple: intentionality from resonance across
horizons, temporality from ordered horizon-translation, perspective from a home region in
the horizon category, differentiation from suppression, and reflexion from self-application
of the horizon functor.

## Chapter summary

John Dewey therefore enters idea theory as a disciplined local algebra of meaning. The
chapter has followed the promised path: biography, position, algebraic re-statement, theorem
re-statement, addition, divergence, horizon structure, and derived primitives. The result is
not a replacement for the thinker, but a listening guide to the formal pressure already
present in the work.


# Chapter 68 — Gottlob Frege

This chapter treats Gottlob Frege as a test case for idea theory. We keep the operating
definitions audible: composition puts ideas together, the identity idea leaves a composition
unchanged, the resonance pairing measures directed evocation, the preservation part,
suppression part, and emergence part describe what is kept, cancelled, and newly produced,
and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — lets the
whole algebra vary by context.

## Biographical preamble

Gottlob Frege, pronounced approximately *GOT-lohb FRAY-guh* lived from 1848 to 1925, worked
in Germany, and belongs here to logic and early analytic philosophy. In one compressed
sentence, he taught that words have meaning only in the context of a proposition. That
sentence gives the chapter its entry point: meaning is not a bare object sitting before a
spectator, but a structured achievement within a field of acts, signs, practices, or
horizons.

## Position

The position can be put simply. Frege founds modern logic with the Begriffsschrift (1879), a
concept-script that supersedes Aristotelian syllogistic with quantification, function-
argument structure, and a rigorous notation for proof. The aim is not new in spirit but
unprecedented in execution: a logic adequate to the inferential structure of mathematics.
The Grundlagen der Arithmetik (1884) and Grundgesetze (1893/1903) pursue the logicist
thesis: arithmetic is reducible to pure logic. Numbers are not abstractions from collections
of physical objects but logical objects, defined as extensions of concepts. The natural
numbers are constructed in the logic itself, with no appeal to intuition. The point is not
merely historical. It tells us what kind of item is being counted as an idea: not a loose
mental image, but a content whose sense depends on the way it is given, used, interpreted,
or carried forward.

## Algebraic re-statement

In the algebraic re-statement, the basic domain is the field of contents named by the zoo
entry. Take the idea domain to be the set of senses (Sinne): items as modes of presentation
of references, finer-grained than references themselves and capable of being grasped by
thought. Each element is tagged by its category (object-sense, concept-sense, relation-
sense) and by its logical complexity. The identity idea is a designator-position that admits
any sense of the appropriate type — a logical placeholder for substitution. Composition is
functional application: a concept-sense composed with an object-sense yields a thought-sense
(the sense of the resulting sentence). composition is type-respecting: only senses of
compatible categories can be composed, and the result inherits a determinate type from the
constituents. The resonance pairing is co-reference: the resonance pairing when the content
under discussion and the relevant content have the same Bedeutung. Read aloud, this
says that the thinker supplies a local model of the ten-tuple: a domain of contents, a
composition rule, an identity idea, directed resonance, the threefold division into
preservation, suppression, and emergence, an emergence cocycle, a degree of articulation,
and a context-sensitive horizon structure.

## Theorem re-statement

*The theorem-style claim is this: The first theorem-shaped claim is that sense is the primary element of the idea domain; reference is the value of the emergence part.*

In paragraph form, the claim rules out the fantasy that meaning is already complete before
context enters. The first theorem-shaped claim is that sense is the primary element of the
idea domain; reference is the value of the emergence part. The algebra operates on senses;
references are derived. This is a the conjugation theorem claim with the further content that the same reference
can be reached by many distinct senses. The second claim is that co-reference is the
equivalence relation induced by the resonance pairing. The prediction is that failures of
understanding will not be accidental noise. They will cluster where a local content cannot
be carried unchanged across a change of horizon, or where an apparent identity hides
suppressed difference.

Carefully restated, the relevant idea algebra has a domain of contents, an identity idea,
associative composition, the resonance pairing, the preservation part, the suppression part,
the emergence part, the emergence cocycle, degree, the horizon category, and the horizon
functor. The central claim says that the philosopher's privileged object is stable only when
these roles cooperate. Where local data can be glued, the chapter instantiates the profundity theorem; where
translation fails, the obstruction is also audible as a horizon-functor effect.

## What the formalism adds

What the formalism adds is clarification without replacement. First, the sense-reference
distinction becomes a fibre-bundle structure: the projection is the relevant formal role, and
senses are fibres over references. Different senses over the same reference are elements
with the resonance pairing but with its location in the idea domain. The pictorial
geometry of fibres makes Frege's distinction structurally vivid. Second, the
compositionality of sense becomes the algebraic claim that composition preserves the
relevant structure: the sense of a complex expression is determined by the senses of its
parts and the way they are combined. The Fregean slogan gains an algebraic correlate. In
ordinary language, the formalism lets us ask which operation does the philosophical work. Is
the key relation resonance, because one content fulfills or evokes another? Is it
suppression, because meaning appears through contrast? Is it the horizon functor, because
every local act changes when the context changes? The answer differs from thinker to
thinker, but the vocabulary stays stable.

## Where it diverges

The divergence is equally important. First, Frege's Context Principle (only in sentence
context does a word have reference) means composition must be holistic; the formalism allows
any composition, which is too liberal. The Fregean discipline of analyzing top-down rather
than bottom-up is hard to enforce structurally. Second, the third realm of senses —
abstract, mind-independent, eternal — has a Platonist ontology the algebra is silent about.
the idea domain is a structural object; whether senses inhabit a third realm is a
metaphysical question outside the structural facts. Third, the strict distinction between
concept (unsaturated, function-like) and object (saturated, argument-like) is more delicate
than the algebra's category-tagging captures. This is not a defect in the reading. It marks
the price of any formal reconstruction: the algebra shows the load-bearing structure, while
the original philosophy often also includes tone, method, ethical urgency, religious
practice, literary performance, or first-person discipline.

## Horizon structure and the horizon-functor theorem

The horizon structure names the context in which the local algebra lives. Frege's the
horizon category is the logical space of senses, references, functions, arguments, and
judgment-strokes, sharply separated from psychology. Morphisms translate ordinary
propositions into Begriffsschrift, preserving inferential articulation while stripping away
subjective association. rigid reference is reference, intentionality is sense as mode of
presentation, and differentiation separates concept and object by type-theoretic role rather
than empirical contrast. This is the profundity theorem: a thought's objectivity is its trans-horizonal
stability across thinkers, languages, and presentations once expressed in logical form. For
this reason the chapter should be heard through the profundity theorem. The hermeneutic sheaf theorem is the hermeneutic sheaf theorem,
where local meanings glue into a global one when they agree on overlaps. The indexical pullback theorem is the
indexical pullback theorem, where preservation and emergence fail to translate cleanly. The profundity theorem
is the profundity theorem, where depth comes from breadth across horizons and closure under
emergence.

## Derived primitives

The derived primitives make the diagnosis compact. rigid reference: Bedeutung supplies
reference, with proper names rigidly aiming at objects rather than senses. intentionality:
Sinn is the mode of presentation through which a reference is intentionally given.
differentiation: The concept/object distinction differentiates predicative functions from
saturated objects. credence: Truth-values are objects that stabilize judgment's credence
target as the True or the False. None of these is introduced as an extra metaphysical
ingredient. Each is derived from the ten-tuple: intentionality from resonance across
horizons, temporality from ordered horizon-translation, perspective from a home region in
the horizon category, differentiation from suppression, and reflexion from self-application
of the horizon functor.

## Chapter summary

Gottlob Frege therefore enters idea theory as a disciplined local algebra of meaning. The
chapter has followed the promised path: biography, position, algebraic re-statement, theorem
re-statement, addition, divergence, horizon structure, and derived primitives. The result is
not a replacement for the thinker, but a listening guide to the formal pressure already
present in the work.


# Chapter 69 — Bertrand Russell

This chapter treats Bertrand Russell as a test case for idea theory. We keep the operating
definitions audible: composition puts ideas together, the identity idea leaves a composition
unchanged, the resonance pairing measures directed evocation, the preservation part,
suppression part, and emergence part describe what is kept, cancelled, and newly produced,
and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — lets the
whole algebra vary by context.

## Biographical preamble

Bertrand Russell, pronounced approximately *RUSS-ul* lived from 1872 to 1970, worked in
Britain, and belongs here to logic, analytic philosophy, and public reason. In one
compressed sentence, he sought clarity by beginning with what analysis can make simple. That
sentence gives the chapter its entry point: meaning is not a bare object sitting before a
spectator, but a structured achievement within a field of acts, signs, practices, or
horizons.

## Position

The position can be put simply. Russell's mature analytic project takes logical analysis as
the master method of philosophy. Apparent ontological commitments — to the present King of
France, to round squares, to classes — are dissolved by showing that the offending
expressions are "incomplete symbols" whose surface grammar misleads us about their logical
form. The Theory of Descriptions ("On Denoting", 1905) is the paradigm. "The present King of
France is bald" appears to attribute baldness to a non-existent monarch; analysis reveals it
as a quantified statement — there exists a unique x who presently kings France and who is
bald — that is simply false (because no such x exists), without commitment to a referent.
Principia Mathematica (with Whitehead, 1910–1913) attempts the logicist reduction of
mathematics to logic, repaired against Russell's own paradox by the introduction of type
theory. The point is not merely historical. It tells us what kind of item is being counted
as an idea: not a loose mental image, but a content whose sense depends on the way it is
given, used, interpreted, or carried forward.

## Algebraic re-statement

In the algebraic re-statement, the basic domain is the field of contents named by the zoo
entry. Take the idea domain to be the set of logical forms or propositional functions: items
as their logical structure exposed by analysis, not their grammatical surface. Each element
is tagged by its type level, its quantificational structure, and its predicate-argument
decomposition. The identity idea is the empty propositional position — the slot a
propositional function fills when its arguments are supplied. Composition is logical
composition: conjunction, disjunction, conditional, quantification, application of
propositional functions to arguments. composition is type-respecting; combinations that
violate the type stratification are not legal compositions. The resonance pairing is
implication strength: the degree to which one logical form entails another, with full
entailment as the limiting case. Read aloud, this says that the thinker supplies a local
model of the ten-tuple: a domain of contents, a composition rule, an identity idea, directed
resonance, the threefold division into preservation, suppression, and emergence, an
emergence cocycle, a degree of articulation, and a context-sensitive horizon structure.

## Theorem re-statement

*The theorem-style claim is this: The first theorem-shaped claim is that the Theory of Descriptions is the emergence part: an analytic operation that maps apparent names to quantified structures.*

In paragraph form, the claim rules out the fantasy that meaning is already complete before
context enters. The first theorem-shaped claim is that the Theory of Descriptions is the
emergence part: an analytic operation that maps apparent names to quantified structures. The
Russellian success is that the mapping eliminates ontological commitment to the apparent
referent. This is a the conjugation theorem claim with major metaphysical leverage. The second claim is that type
theory is the grading degree that prevents Russell's paradox. The prediction is that
failures of understanding will not be accidental noise. They will cluster where a local
content cannot be carried unchanged across a change of horizon, or where an apparent
identity hides suppressed difference.

Carefully restated, the relevant idea algebra has a domain of contents, an identity idea,
associative composition, the resonance pairing, the preservation part, the suppression part,
the emergence part, the emergence cocycle, degree, the horizon category, and the horizon
functor. The central claim says that the philosopher's privileged object is stable only when
these roles cooperate. Where local data can be glued, the chapter instantiates the indexical pullback theorem; where
translation fails, the obstruction is also audible as a horizon-functor effect.

## What the formalism adds

What the formalism adds is clarification without replacement. First, type theory as grading
(the graded structure theorem) explains why the paradox disappears: the preservation part is 0 within each type level
because the self-referential composition is blocked by degree. The hierarchy enables
consistency without renouncing the logicist project. Second, the Theory of Descriptions
becomes a precise structural transformation rather than a clever paraphrase. the emergence
part takes one element and returns another, with definite logical properties; the analysis
is repeatable and checkable. Third, the elimination of incomplete symbols becomes the
algebraic claim that not every grammatical surface corresponds to an element of the idea
domain. In ordinary language, the formalism lets us ask which operation does the
philosophical work. Is the key relation resonance, because one content fulfills or evokes
another? Is it suppression, because meaning appears through contrast? Is it the horizon
functor, because every local act changes when the context changes? The answer differs from
thinker to thinker, but the vocabulary stays stable.

## Where it diverges

The divergence is equally important. First, Russell's multiple-relation theory of judgment
(briefly held, then abandoned) and his neutral monism are not captured; the formalism
assumes the idea domain consists of logical forms, not sensibilia or neutral elements.
Russell's metaphysical experimentation is wider than the algebra accommodates. Second, the
axiom of reducibility, which Russell needed to make ramified type theory mathematically
adequate, has the air of an ad hoc fix. The algebra inherits the awkwardness: ensuring sub-
algebras for ordinary mathematics may require analogous patching. Third, Russell's
epistemological work on knowledge by acquaintance and knowledge by description introduces a
psychological-epistemological dimension that the algebra cannot honor. This is not a defect
in the reading. It marks the price of any formal reconstruction: the algebra shows the load-
bearing structure, while the original philosophy often also includes tone, method, ethical
urgency, religious practice, literary performance, or first-person discipline.

## Horizon structure and the horizon-functor theorem

The horizon structure names the context in which the local algebra lives. Russell's the
horizon category includes acquaintance, description, logical analysis, type hierarchy, and
scientific construction. Morphisms replace misleading grammatical form with logical form,
especially by eliminating denoting phrases into quantificational structure. rigid reference
is restricted to objects of acquaintance, R diagnoses self-reference and paradox,
differentiation separates types, and credence appears in epistemic constructions from sense-
data. This is the indexical pullback theorem: ordinary grammar fails to pull back into logical form without
obstruction, and the theory of descriptions is the controlled translation that removes it.
For this reason the chapter should be heard through the indexical pullback theorem. The hermeneutic sheaf theorem is the hermeneutic sheaf
theorem, where local meanings glue into a global one when they agree on overlaps. The indexical pullback theorem is the
indexical pullback theorem, where preservation and emergence fail to translate cleanly. The profundity theorem
is the profundity theorem, where depth comes from breadth across horizons and closure under
emergence.

## Derived primitives

The derived primitives make the diagnosis compact. R. Russell's paradox exposes failed
reflexion when a class is allowed to contain itself. differentiation: Type theory
differentiates levels to block vicious self-application and semantic collapse. rigid
reference: Logical atoms and acquaintance supply direct reference where descriptions are
analyzed away. intentionality: Definite descriptions show apparent aboutness can be
decomposed into quantificational structure. None of these is introduced as an extra
metaphysical ingredient. Each is derived from the ten-tuple: intentionality from resonance
across horizons, temporality from ordered horizon-translation, perspective from a home
region in the horizon category, differentiation from suppression, and reflexion from self-
application of the horizon functor.

## Chapter summary

Bertrand Russell therefore enters idea theory as a disciplined local algebra of meaning. The
chapter has followed the promised path: biography, position, algebraic re-statement, theorem
re-statement, addition, divergence, horizon structure, and derived primitives. The result is
not a replacement for the thinker, but a listening guide to the formal pressure already
present in the work.


# Chapter 70 — Early Wittgenstein

This chapter treats Early Wittgenstein as a test case for idea theory. We keep the operating
definitions audible: composition puts ideas together, the identity idea leaves a composition
unchanged, the resonance pairing measures directed evocation, the preservation part,
suppression part, and emergence part describe what is kept, cancelled, and newly produced,
and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — lets the
whole algebra vary by context.

## Biographical preamble

Ludwig Wittgenstein, pronounced approximately *LOOD-vig VIT-gen-shtine* lived from 1889 to
1951, worked in Austria and Britain, and belongs here to early analytic philosophy. In one
compressed sentence, he wrote that the limits of language mean the limits of the world. That
sentence gives the chapter its entry point: meaning is not a bare object sitting before a
spectator, but a structured achievement within a field of acts, signs, practices, or
horizons.

## Position

The position can be put simply. The Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus (1921) presents an
austere picture: the world is the totality of facts, not of things; facts are configurations
of objects; elementary propositions are pictures of atomic facts; molecular propositions are
truth-functional combinations of elementary ones. Logic takes care of itself: the rules of
inference are tautologies that say nothing about the world. The picture theory of meaning
grounds the proposition's representational power: a proposition shares with the fact it
pictures a logical form, and depicting consists in representing one structure by another
with the same logical form. To understand a proposition is to grasp this isomorphism.
Logical analysis decomposes complex propositions into their elementary constituents. The
point is not merely historical. It tells us what kind of item is being counted as an idea:
not a loose mental image, but a content whose sense depends on the way it is given, used,
interpreted, or carried forward.

## Algebraic re-statement

In the algebraic re-statement, the basic domain is the field of contents named by the zoo
entry. Take the idea domain to be the set of elementary propositions (and their truth-
functional combinations): items insofar as they picture atomic facts. Each element is tagged
by its truth-functional decomposition and by the elementary constituents from which it is
built. The identity idea is the tautology: the proposition true under every truth-
assignment, contributing no information. Composition is truth-functional composition:
conjunction, disjunction, negation, conditional. composition is governed entirely by the
propositional calculus; no extra-logical principles enter. The algebra is in this sense
maximally clean. Read aloud, this says that the thinker supplies a local model of the ten-
tuple: a domain of contents, a composition rule, an identity idea, directed resonance, the
threefold division into preservation, suppression, and emergence, an emergence cocycle, a
degree of articulation, and a context-sensitive horizon structure.

## Theorem re-statement

*The theorem-style claim is this: The first theorem-shaped claim is that the world is flat: the preservation part is 0 everywhere.*

In paragraph form, the claim rules out the fantasy that meaning is already complete before
context enters. The first theorem-shaped claim is that the world is flat: the preservation
part is 0 everywhere. All logical structure is tautological; logic adds nothing to the world
but exhibits the form already there. This is a the meaning curvature theorem claim with negative valence: the algebra
has no irreducible curvature. The second claim is that truth-functions are composition: the
only legitimate composition is truth-functional, and any apparent non-truth-functional
combination is either further analyzable into truth-functional form or is nonsensical. The
prediction is that failures of understanding will not be accidental noise. They will cluster
where a local content cannot be carried unchanged across a change of horizon, or where an
apparent identity hides suppressed difference.

Carefully restated, the relevant idea algebra has a domain of contents, an identity idea,
associative composition, the resonance pairing, the preservation part, the suppression part,
the emergence part, the emergence cocycle, degree, the horizon category, and the horizon
functor. The central claim says that the philosopher's privileged object is stable only when
these roles cooperate. Where local data can be glued, the chapter instantiates the indexical pullback theorem; where
translation fails, the obstruction is also audible as a horizon-functor effect.

## What the formalism adds

What the formalism adds is clarification without replacement. First, the picture theory
becomes the claim that the resonance pairing is maximal when the proposition is true — a
precise correspondence theory in which truth is structural isomorphism between proposition
and fact. Second, the preservation part is 0 makes explicit the claim that logic adds
nothing to the world. The algebra of the Tractatus is structurally unique among the
philosopher zoo: it is the only one that requires zero curvature throughout, and the
requirement has dramatic consequences. Third, the showing/saying distinction becomes the
structural distinction between what is internal to the algebra (the elements and their
compositions, which can be said) and what is internal to the algebra's form (the categorial
constraints on composition and the type structure, which cannot be said but show themselves
in the algebra's operation). Fourth, the limits of language correspond to the boundary of
the idea domain. In ordinary language, the formalism lets us ask which operation does the
philosophical work. Is the key relation resonance, because one content fulfills or evokes
another? Is it suppression, because meaning appears through contrast? Is it the horizon
functor, because every local act changes when the context changes? The answer differs from
thinker to thinker, but the vocabulary stays stable.

## Where it diverges

The divergence is equally important. First, the showing/saying distinction — what shows
itself but cannot be said — corresponds to what is outside the idea domain but constitutive
of it; the formalism has no native way to represent this. The structural form of the algebra
shows in its operation, but the algebra cannot describe its own form from within. Second,
the picture theory presupposes a notion of logical form shared between proposition and fact
that the algebra does not internally contain. The algebra's elements are propositional;
facts are extra-algebraic, and the isomorphism that gives meaning lives at the interface.
Third, the elementary propositions on which the entire structure rests were never actually
exhibited by Wittgenstein; their existence is a postulate. This is not a defect in the
reading. It marks the price of any formal reconstruction: the algebra shows the load-bearing
structure, while the original philosophy often also includes tone, method, ethical urgency,
religious practice, literary performance, or first-person discipline.

## Horizon structure and the horizon-functor theorem

The horizon structure names the context in which the local algebra lives. The early
Wittgenstein's the horizon category is the austere picture-theoretic space of facts,
elementary propositions, logical form, and the limit of language. Morphisms project
propositions onto possible states of affairs when they share pictorial form; ethical,
aesthetic, and metaphysical utterances lack such admissible projection. rigid reference
binds names to objects, schema or paradigm is logical space, and R appears when the book's
own propositions must be thrown away. This is the indexical pullback theorem: what can be shown does not pull back into
sayable propositions, so metaphysical sense fails at the boundary of the horizon category.
For this reason the chapter should be heard through the indexical pullback theorem. The hermeneutic sheaf theorem is the hermeneutic sheaf
theorem, where local meanings glue into a global one when they agree on overlaps. The indexical pullback theorem is the
indexical pullback theorem, where preservation and emergence fail to translate cleanly. The profundity theorem
is the profundity theorem, where depth comes from breadth across horizons and closure under
emergence.

## Derived primitives

The derived primitives make the diagnosis compact. rigid reference: Names stand for simple
objects, providing rigid anchors within logical space. schema or paradigm: Logical space is
the schema of possible states of affairs that propositions partition. intentionality: The
picture relation makes propositions intentionally about facts by sharing logical form. R.
The ladder is reflexive: the book shows its own propositions must finally be thrown away.
None of these is introduced as an extra metaphysical ingredient. Each is derived from the
ten-tuple: intentionality from resonance across horizons, temporality from ordered horizon-
translation, perspective from a home region in the horizon category, differentiation from
suppression, and reflexion from self-application of the horizon functor.

## Chapter summary

Early Wittgenstein therefore enters idea theory as a disciplined local algebra of meaning.
The chapter has followed the promised path: biography, position, algebraic re-statement,
theorem re-statement, addition, divergence, horizon structure, and derived primitives. The
result is not a replacement for the thinker, but a listening guide to the formal pressure
already present in the work.


# Chapter 71 — Late Wittgenstein

This chapter treats Late Wittgenstein as a test case for idea theory. We keep the operating
definitions audible: composition puts ideas together, the identity idea leaves a composition
unchanged, the resonance pairing measures directed evocation, the preservation part,
suppression part, and emergence part describe what is kept, cancelled, and newly produced,
and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — lets the
whole algebra vary by context.

## Biographical preamble

Ludwig Wittgenstein, pronounced approximately *LOOD-vig VIT-gen-shtine* lived from 1889 to
1951, worked in Austria and Britain, and belongs here to ordinary-language philosophy. In
one compressed sentence, his later watchword was that meaning is use. That sentence gives
the chapter its entry point: meaning is not a bare object sitting before a spectator, but a
structured achievement within a field of acts, signs, practices, or horizons.

## Position

The position can be put simply. The Philosophical Investigations (1953) repudiates the
picture theory of the Tractatus . Meaning is not a relation of word to object or proposition
to fact but a feature of use within a practice. To understand a word is to know how it is
used; to ask for its meaning apart from its use is to be misled by a grammatical picture.
Language games are the units of analysis: small, complete forms of language-with-action that
exhibit how words function. The slab-game of the builders, the giving-and-following of
orders, the reporting of pain — each is a language game with its own internal grammar. The
point is not merely historical. It tells us what kind of item is being counted as an idea:
not a loose mental image, but a content whose sense depends on the way it is given, used,
interpreted, or carried forward.

## Algebraic re-statement

In the algebraic re-statement, the basic domain is the field of contents named by the zoo
entry. Take the idea domain to be the set of language games: practices in which words and
actions are interwoven into complete forms of life. Each element is tagged by the game it
belongs to, the role it plays in that game, and the criteria of correct use that govern it.
The identity idea is the empty practice-position — the slot a contribution to a language
game fills. Composition is language-game combination: the way smaller games are interwoven
into larger forms of life. composition is constrained by the forms of life themselves; not
every combination is a recognizable practice, and the constraints are themselves part of the
form of life. The resonance pairing is family resemblance: an overlap measure of features
shared between practices, with no requirement that any single feature be common to all. Read
aloud, this says that the thinker supplies a local model of the ten-tuple: a domain of
contents, a composition rule, an identity idea, directed resonance, the threefold division
into preservation, suppression, and emergence, an emergence cocycle, a degree of
articulation, and a context-sensitive horizon structure.

## Theorem re-statement

*The theorem-style claim is this: The first theorem-shaped claim is that meaning is use: there is no element of the idea domain apart from its role in composition.*

In paragraph form, the claim rules out the fantasy that meaning is already complete before
context enters. The first theorem-shaped claim is that meaning is use: there is no element
of the idea domain apart from its role in composition. To pull a word out of its language
game and ask for its meaning in isolation is to encounter no meaning at all. This is a the composition theorem
claim with extreme holism: identity of an element is exhausted by its compositional role.
The second claim is that family resemblance is the resonance pairing as an overlap count,
not an essence. The prediction is that failures of understanding will not be accidental
noise. They will cluster where a local content cannot be carried unchanged across a change
of horizon, or where an apparent identity hides suppressed difference.

Carefully restated, the relevant idea algebra has a domain of contents, an identity idea,
associative composition, the resonance pairing, the preservation part, the suppression part,
the emergence part, the emergence cocycle, degree, the horizon category, and the horizon
functor. The central claim says that the philosopher's privileged object is stable only when
these roles cooperate. Where local data can be glued, the chapter instantiates the indexical pullback theorem; where
translation fails, the obstruction is also audible as a horizon-functor effect.

## What the formalism adds

What the formalism adds is clarification without replacement. First, family resemblance
becomes a precise resonance metric without essential properties. The intuition
that some concepts hang together without sharing an essence gains an algebraic correlate:
the resonance pairing is overlap-based rather than membership-in-a-class-based. Second, the
private language argument becomes the assertion that the idea domain requires a public
composition to be well-defined — a social condition on the monoid. The argument's structural
form is that without a public composition, there are no determinate elements to be composed.
Third, the rejection of essences is the structural claim that composition does not factor
through any privileged set of features. In ordinary language, the formalism lets us ask
which operation does the philosophical work. Is the key relation resonance, because one
content fulfills or evokes another? Is it suppression, because meaning appears through
contrast? Is it the horizon functor, because every local act changes when the context
changes? The answer differs from thinker to thinker, but the vocabulary stays stable.

## Where it diverges

The divergence is equally important. First, the anti-theoretical stance of the
Investigations directly opposes building a theory; the formalism produces exactly the kind
of theory Wittgenstein II opposes. To present an algebra of language games risks performing
the very mistake the book diagnoses. Second, the piecemeal, conversational, dialectical
style of the Investigations is part of its content. The algebra's systematic exposition
flattens what Wittgenstein deliberately presents as a series of remarks intended to provoke
rather than to demonstrate. Third, the absence of theses in Wittgenstein's later philosophy
is in tension with the theorem-shaped claims the algebra invites. This is not a defect in
the reading. It marks the price of any formal reconstruction: the algebra shows the load-
bearing structure, while the original philosophy often also includes tone, method, ethical
urgency, religious practice, literary performance, or first-person discipline.

## Horizon structure and the horizon-functor theorem

The horizon structure names the context in which the local algebra lives. The later
Wittgenstein's the horizon category is the discrete-up-to-family-resemblance category of
language-games and forms of life. Morphisms are partial overlaps in use, training, and
criteria, not hidden common essences; meaning is intra-horizonal and public. schema or
paradigm is the form of life, perspective is the participant trained into rule-following,
differentiation is contrastive use, and private-language fantasy is a failed singleton
horizon. This is paradigmatic the indexical pullback theorem: cross-game inference fails, and the private-language
argument denies that any the horizon functor-section over an isolated private horizon is
even definable. For this reason the chapter should be heard through the indexical pullback theorem. The hermeneutic sheaf theorem is the
hermeneutic sheaf theorem, where local meanings glue into a global one when they agree on
overlaps. The indexical pullback theorem is the indexical pullback theorem, where preservation and emergence fail to
translate cleanly. The profundity theorem is the profundity theorem, where depth comes from breadth across
horizons and closure under emergence.

## Derived primitives

The derived primitives make the diagnosis compact. schema or paradigm: Language-games are
connected components, each giving rules for locally intelligible use. differentiation:
Meaning-as-use is differential, fixed by contrasts among practices rather than inner
objects. perspective: Form of life is the shared perspective from which rule-following
becomes possible. R. Philosophical therapy is reflexive, dissolving confusions generated by
our own language. None of these is introduced as an extra metaphysical ingredient. Each is
derived from the ten-tuple: intentionality from resonance across horizons, temporality from
ordered horizon-translation, perspective from a home region in the horizon category,
differentiation from suppression, and reflexion from self-application of the horizon
functor.

## Chapter summary

Late Wittgenstein therefore enters idea theory as a disciplined local algebra of meaning.
The chapter has followed the promised path: biography, position, algebraic re-statement,
theorem re-statement, addition, divergence, horizon structure, and derived primitives. The
result is not a replacement for the thinker, but a listening guide to the formal pressure
already present in the work.


# Chapter 72 — Willard Van Orman Quine

This chapter treats Willard Van Orman Quine as a test case for idea theory. We keep the
operating definitions audible: composition puts ideas together, the identity idea leaves a
composition unchanged, the resonance pairing measures directed evocation, the preservation
part, suppression part, and emergence part describe what is kept, cancelled, and newly
produced, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi —
lets the whole algebra vary by context.

## Biographical preamble

Willard Van Orman Quine, pronounced approximately *kwine* lived from 1908 to 2000, worked in
the United States, and belongs here to analytic philosophy and naturalized epistemology. In
one compressed sentence, he argued that no statement is immune to revision. That sentence
gives the chapter its entry point: meaning is not a bare object sitting before a spectator,
but a structured achievement within a field of acts, signs, practices, or horizons.

## Position

The position can be put simply. Quine's "Two Dogmas of Empiricism" (1951) attacks the
analytic-synthetic distinction and the verificationist atomism on which the early logical
positivists relied. There is no principled way to separate truths in virtue of meaning from
truths in virtue of fact; analyticity, once examined, dissolves into a circle of equally
suspect notions. The positive thesis is confirmation holism: the unit of empirical
significance is the whole of science, not individual statements. When a theory clashes with
experience, no single statement is uniquely refuted; we may save any statement at the cost
of revising others, and the overall web of belief is what is empirically constrained.
Ontology is relative to a theory. The point is not merely historical. It tells us what kind
of item is being counted as an idea: not a loose mental image, but a content whose sense
depends on the way it is given, used, interpreted, or carried forward.

## Algebraic re-statement

In the algebraic re-statement, the basic domain is the field of contents named by the zoo
entry. Take the idea domain to be the set of sentences of a total scientific theory: items
as standing claims integrated into the web of belief. Each element is tagged by its
centrality (logical, mathematical, theoretical, observational) and by its empirical
exposure. The identity idea is the trivially-true sentence — what every theory accepts
because it makes no claim. Composition is deductive closure plus integration: the addition
of sentences to the web in such a way that the new web remains coherent. composition is
constrained by the requirement that the resulting set be consistent and adequate to the data
the theory must accommodate. The resonance pairing is degree of mutual dependence in the web
of belief: how strongly the truth of the relevant content is implicated by the truth
of the relevant content and vice versa. Read aloud, this says that the thinker
supplies a local model of the ten-tuple: a domain of contents, a composition rule, an
identity idea, directed resonance, the threefold division into preservation, suppression,
and emergence, an emergence cocycle, a degree of articulation, and a context-sensitive
horizon structure.

## Theorem re-statement

*The theorem-style claim is this: The first theorem-shaped claim is holism: every sentence's content depends on all others via the resonance pairing.*

In paragraph form, the claim rules out the fantasy that meaning is already complete before
context enters. The first theorem-shaped claim is holism: every sentence's content depends
on all others via the resonance pairing. There are no isolated atoms of content; meaning is
global. This is a the resonance theorem claim with maximal scope. The second claim is that the web of belief is
the connected algebra under composition. The prediction is that failures of understanding
will not be accidental noise. They will cluster where a local content cannot be carried
unchanged across a change of horizon, or where an apparent identity hides suppressed
difference.

Carefully restated, the relevant idea algebra has a domain of contents, an identity idea,
associative composition, the resonance pairing, the preservation part, the suppression part,
the emergence part, the emergence cocycle, degree, the horizon category, and the horizon
functor. The central claim says that the philosopher's privileged object is stable only when
these roles cooperate. Where local data can be glued, the chapter instantiates the indexical pullback theorem; where
translation fails, the obstruction is also audible as a horizon-functor effect.

## What the formalism adds

What the formalism adds is clarification without replacement. First, the centrality degree
gives precise content to Quine's claim that logic and mathematics are more central. Higher
degree means more resistant to revision; the inertia of the central is structurally
measured. Second, ontological relativity is the assertion that the idea domain and the idea
domain can be proxy-related while being non-isomorphic with respect to the objects their
quantifiers range over. The algebra exhibits the relativity as a structural fact: theories
with the same observational consequences may differ in their algebraic shape. Third, the
indeterminacy of translation becomes the absence of a unique the emergence mapping one
theory's regimentation onto another's. In ordinary language, the formalism lets us ask which
operation does the philosophical work. Is the key relation resonance, because one content
fulfills or evokes another? Is it suppression, because meaning appears through contrast? Is
it the horizon functor, because every local act changes when the context changes? The answer
differs from thinker to thinker, but the vocabulary stays stable.

## Where it diverges

The divergence is equally important. First, Quine's behaviorism about meaning — meaning as
stimulus meaning, accessible only through observable response to stimuli — is not captured
by the algebraic the resonance pairing, which is theory-internal. The algebra describes the
web; the behaviorist account of how we get into the web requires extra-algebraic resources.
Second, Quine's sweeping eliminativism about modality, intentionality, and abstract meanings
is more than the algebra requires. The structural facts can be honored without the
metaphysical denial; nothing in the algebra forces Quine's revisionary austerity. Third, the
indeterminacy of translation, taken at its strongest, threatens the very identity of the
algebra. This is not a defect in the reading. It marks the price of any formal
reconstruction: the algebra shows the load-bearing structure, while the original philosophy
often also includes tone, method, ethical urgency, religious practice, literary performance,
or first-person discipline.

## Horizon structure and the horizon-functor theorem

The horizon structure names the context in which the local algebra lives. Quine's the
horizon category is the web of belief under sensory stimulation, translation manuals,
ontological commitments, and regimentation into canonical notation. Morphisms revise the web
holistically or translate another language by matching stimulus meanings, but there is no
unique fact selecting one manual. schema or paradigm is the conceptual scheme only insofar
as it is a connected web, credence is empirical confidence distributed over the web, and
rigid reference is relativised to background theory. This is the indexical pullback theorem: translation and ontology
fail to pull back uniquely across manuals, with indeterminacy appearing as a first
cohomological obstruction in interpretation. For this reason the chapter should be heard
through the indexical pullback theorem. The hermeneutic sheaf theorem is the hermeneutic sheaf theorem, where local meanings glue into a global
one when they agree on overlaps. The indexical pullback theorem is the indexical pullback theorem, where preservation
and emergence fail to translate cleanly. The profundity theorem is the profundity theorem, where depth comes
from breadth across horizons and closure under emergence.

## Derived primitives

The derived primitives make the diagnosis compact. schema or paradigm: The web of belief is
a schema whose central and peripheral strands revise differently. differentiation:
Indeterminacy of translation makes meanings differential across manuals compatible with all
behavioral evidence. rigid reference: Inscrutability of reference denies uniquely rigid
bindings beneath equally adequate translation schemes. credence: Naturalized epistemology
treats credence as continuous with empirical psychology and prediction. None of these is
introduced as an extra metaphysical ingredient. Each is derived from the ten-tuple:
intentionality from resonance across horizons, temporality from ordered horizon-translation,
perspective from a home region in the horizon category, differentiation from suppression,
and reflexion from self-application of the horizon functor.

## Chapter summary

Willard Van Orman Quine therefore enters idea theory as a disciplined local algebra of
meaning. The chapter has followed the promised path: biography, position, algebraic re-
statement, theorem re-statement, addition, divergence, horizon structure, and derived
primitives. The result is not a replacement for the thinker, but a listening guide to the
formal pressure already present in the work.


# Chapter 73 — Jerry Fodor

This chapter treats Jerry Fodor as a test case for idea theory. We keep the operating
definitions audible: composition puts ideas together, the identity idea leaves a composition
unchanged, the resonance pairing measures directed evocation, the preservation part,
suppression part, and emergence part describe what is kept, cancelled, and newly produced,
and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — lets the
whole algebra vary by context.

## Biographical preamble

Jerry Fodor, pronounced approximately *FOH-dor* lived from 1935 to 2017, worked in the
United States, and belongs here to philosophy of mind and cognitive science. In one
compressed sentence, he defended the view that thought has a language-like structure. That
sentence gives the chapter its entry point: meaning is not a bare object sitting before a
spectator, but a structured achievement within a field of acts, signs, practices, or
horizons.

## Position

The position can be put simply. Fodor argues for a representational and computational theory
of mind. Cognitive processes are formal operations performed over syntactically structured
mental representations, and these representations form a language of thought (Mentalese)
with the productivity, systematicity, and compositionality that natural languages display.
The central argument from systematicity is that anyone who can think aRb can also think bRa:
thoughts come in systematically related families, and the best explanation of this fact is
that thoughts have constituent structure — they are built up from atomic concepts by
combinatorial operations. Modularity of mind, in The Modularity of Mind (1983), holds that
perception and language processing are accomplished by encapsulated, domain-specific,
mandatory modules. Higher cognition (central systems) is, by contrast, isotropic and Quinean
— and therefore, Fodor pessimistically concludes, less amenable to scientific theorizing.
The point is not merely historical. It tells us what kind of item is being counted as an
