a shared understanding. The indexical pullback theorem applies when a sign, doctrine, or word fails to pull back
cleanly into another setting. The profundity theorem applies when an insight becomes profound because it
remains fertile across many horizons while continuing to generate new consequences.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds a common grammar without erasing difference. It says that emptiness,
différance, and language-games converge because each makes horizon-relativity constitutive.
It also separates three questions that are often blurred. First, what is the local
composition rule by which terms work together? Second, what gets suppressed when a term is
stabilized? Third, what new content emerges when the term travels? Once those questions are
separated, the traditions can be compared without reducing one to another.

It also clarifies why these doctrines are so easy to misunderstand. If the listener hears
only suppression, emptiness sounds like nihilism, deconstruction sounds like destruction,
and language-games sound like conventionalism. If the listener also hears preservation and
emergence, the picture changes. Each doctrine preserves local intelligibility, suppresses
the fantasy of self-standing essence, and lets new meaning emerge through disciplined
movement across horizons.

## Where it diverges

The divergence is real. Nāgārjuna is not secretly writing twentieth-century European theory,
Derrida is not simply repeating Buddhist emptiness, and Wittgenstein is not offering a
Madhyamaka path under another name. Their aims, vocabularies, and forms of discipline
differ. The algebra abstracts from liberation, textual style, and ordinary therapeutic
method. It also risks making the three doctrines look more systematic than their authors
would allow.

That risk is acceptable only if the comparison remains modest. The claim is not sameness of
content. The claim is convergence at the level of horizon structure. All three traditions
teach that meaning is local without being private, relational without being unreal, and
movable without being perfectly portable.

## Horizon structure and the horizon-functor theorem

The horizon category for this bridge has several connected regions: Buddhist analysis of
dependent origination, post-structuralist textual interpretation, and ordinary language-
games. Some translations between regions are fruitful, and some are obstructed. The hermeneutic sheaf theorem appears
whenever overlapping practices produce shared intelligibility. The indexical pullback theorem appears whenever a word
such as emptiness, trace, or game is mistranslated as a fixed doctrine. The profundity theorem appears because
the deepest insights here survive precisely by traveling across horizons without becoming a
view from nowhere.

## Derived primitives

The diagnostic derived primitives are differentiation, temporality, schema or paradigm,
perspective, and reflexion. Differentiation explains why meaning arises through contrast.
Temporality explains why the sign or practice is never exhausted in a single present moment.
Schema or paradigm explains why local forms of life matter. Perspective explains why no
speaker or interpreter is outside all horizons. Reflexion explains why each doctrine must
turn back upon itself: emptiness is empty, deconstruction deconstructs its own terms, and
language-game analysis is itself one practice among others.

## Chapter summary

The bridge chapter has compared Mahayana emptiness, Derridean différance, and
Wittgensteinian language-games as converging on the horizon functor. Meaning is local,
relational, and transportable only through structured translations. The shared lesson is not
that all traditions say the same thing, but that the same formal pressure reappears wherever
philosophy gives up the dream of a view from nowhere.


# Chapter 82 — The Classical Theory of Concepts

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear The Classical Theory of Concepts as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Edward Smith, born 1940, and Douglas Medin, born 1944, are American cognitive psychologists associated with the experimental study of concepts and categorization. Their key book is Categories and Concepts, published in 1981. A sentence often used to summarize the classical view is this: a concept is a rule that specifies necessary and sufficient conditions for membership. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Edward Smith and Douglas Medin generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of definitions as fixed feature bundles. More plainly, a concept works like a checklist. Bachelor means adult, male, and unmarried. If every required feature is present, the case belongs; if one is missing, it does not. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for classical-theory emphasizes the same structure: The entry presents concepts as necessary and sufficient definitions, with all-or-nothing membership, clean conjunction by feature intersection, no genuine emergence, and a deliberately trivial horizon structure. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For The Classical Theory of Concepts, this means that the plain rule is this: a concept works like a checklist. Bachelor means adult, male, and unmarried. If every required feature is present, the case belongs; if one is missing, it does not. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If the defining bundle is complete, membership is stable across all admissible contexts.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that membership is decided by invariant defining conditions rather than by typicality, context, or use. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Edward Smith and Douglas Medin did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Hermeneutic Sheaf Theorem is engaged only trivially, because the theory assumes one stable definition rather than many local readings that must be glued. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It tells us whether the theory is mainly about gluing local meanings, about obstruction under contextual translation, or about stability across many horizons. That distinction is the algebraic signature of the theory.

## Diagnostic derived primitives

The first diagnostic derived primitive is rigid reference. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is differentiation. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is perspective. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. That is why the chapter can compare a concept theory, a machine-learning architecture, a cultural-evolution model, and an emergence theory without pretending they are the same science.

## Chapter summary

The Classical Theory of Concepts shows how a scientific theory of mind, language, culture, machines, or emergence can be heard as an idea algebra in motion. Its distinctive claim is preserved, but the formalism asks sharper questions: what composes, what resonates, what is preserved, what is suppressed, what emerges, and which horizon changes make the phenomenon appear or disappear.


# Chapter 83 — Eleanor Rosch and Prototype Theory

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Eleanor Rosch and Prototype Theory as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Eleanor Rosch, born in 1938, is an American psychologist whose work reshaped cognitive science in the nineteen seventies. Her central publications include Natural Categories and the studies with Carolyn Mervis on family resemblances. A famous lesson of that work is often paraphrased this way: categories have better and worse examples, not merely members and nonmembers. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Eleanor Rosch generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of prototype centered categorization. More plainly, a concept is organized around its best cases. A robin is a more typical bird than a penguin, and chair is often a more useful level than furniture or rocking chair. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for rosch emphasizes the same structure: The entry treats prototypes as resonance-maximizing members, basic-level categories as cognitively privileged, and prototype shift as a context effect. Pet fish supplies the classic case where a blend has its own emergent typicality. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Eleanor Rosch and Prototype Theory, this means that the plain rule is this: a concept is organized around its best cases. A robin is a more typical bird than a penguin, and chair is often a more useful level than furniture or rocking chair. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If a category has a prototype, then membership confidence falls with decreasing resonance to that prototype, unless a horizon shift changes the active contrast class.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that typicality judgments form graded fields around prototypes and shift with task, culture, and contrast class. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Eleanor Rosch did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Indexical Pullback Theorem is central, because typicality does not move unchanged from backyard birds to arctic birds or from food animals to taxonomic animals. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It tells us whether the theory is mainly about gluing local meanings, about obstruction under contextual translation, or about stability across many horizons. That distinction is the algebraic signature of the theory.

## Diagnostic derived primitives

The first diagnostic derived primitive is credence. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is schema. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is differentiation. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. That is why the chapter can compare a concept theory, a machine-learning architecture, a cultural-evolution model, and an emergence theory without pretending they are the same science.

## Chapter summary

Eleanor Rosch and Prototype Theory shows how a scientific theory of mind, language, culture, machines, or emergence can be heard as an idea algebra in motion. Its distinctive claim is preserved, but the formalism asks sharper questions: what composes, what resonates, what is preserved, what is suppressed, what emerges, and which horizon changes make the phenomenon appear or disappear.


# Chapter 84 — The Theory-Theory of Concepts

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear The Theory-Theory of Concepts as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Alison Gopnik, born in 1955, and Andrew Meltzoff, born in 1950, are American developmental psychologists. Their key book is Words, Thoughts, and Theories, published in 1997. Their guiding claim may be stated this way: children are not passive classifiers but little theorists who revise explanations. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Alison Gopnik and Andrew Meltzoff generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of concepts as nodes in explanatory theories. More plainly, a concept gets its meaning from the miniature theory in which it plays a role. Germ, desire, magnet, and animal are not just feature lists; each belongs to an explanatory network. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for theory-theory emphasizes the same structure: The entry reconstructs concepts as embedded in folk theories, with horizons given by competing explanatory frames. Composition combines theoretical roles, and emergence appears when a new explanation reorganizes old categories. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For The Theory-Theory of Concepts, this means that the plain rule is this: a concept gets its meaning from the miniature theory in which it plays a role. Germ, desire, magnet, and animal are not just feature lists; each belongs to an explanatory network. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If two learners use different background theories, the same word can have different inferential force even when obvious features match.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that conceptual change follows changes in explanatory structure rather than mere changes in similarity. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Alison Gopnik and Andrew Meltzoff did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Hermeneutic Sheaf Theorem is engaged when local observations must be glued into a coherent folk theory; the Indexical Pullback Theorem appears when rival theories block clean translation. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It tells us whether the theory is mainly about gluing local meanings, about obstruction under contextual translation, or about stability across many horizons. That distinction is the algebraic signature of the theory.

## Diagnostic derived primitives

The first diagnostic derived primitive is schema. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is credence. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is potentiality. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. That is why the chapter can compare a concept theory, a machine-learning architecture, a cultural-evolution model, and an emergence theory without pretending they are the same science.

## Chapter summary

The Theory-Theory of Concepts shows how a scientific theory of mind, language, culture, machines, or emergence can be heard as an idea algebra in motion. Its distinctive claim is preserved, but the formalism asks sharper questions: what composes, what resonates, what is preserved, what is suppressed, what emerges, and which horizon changes make the phenomenon appear or disappear.


# Chapter 85 — Lawrence Barsalou and Situated Conceptualization

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Lawrence Barsalou and Situated Conceptualization as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Lawrence Barsalou, born in 1951, is an American cognitive psychologist known for work on ad hoc categories and perceptual symbol systems. A key publication is Perceptual Symbol Systems, published in 1999. His thesis can be compressed into one sentence: concepts are reenactments of perceptual, bodily, and situational experience. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Lawrence Barsalou generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of situated simulation. More plainly, a concept is not a detached dictionary item. It is a capacity to simulate seeing, acting, feeling, and using something in a situation. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for barsalou emphasizes the same structure: The entry presents situated conceptualization as simulation theory, with horizons as bodily and modal contexts. A concept varies as the current task recruits vision, action, affect, memory, or social use. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Lawrence Barsalou and Situated Conceptualization, this means that the plain rule is this: a concept is not a detached dictionary item. It is a capacity to simulate seeing, acting, feeling, and using something in a situation. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If the task horizon changes from eating to repairing to fearing, the same object concept activates different preserved and suppressed features.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that concept activation depends on the situated simulation demanded by a bodily and task horizon. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Lawrence Barsalou did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Indexical Pullback Theorem is most engaged, because conceptual content changes when moved across bodily and task horizons. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It tells us whether the theory is mainly about gluing local meanings, about obstruction under contextual translation, or about stability across many horizons. That distinction is the algebraic signature of the theory.

## Diagnostic derived primitives

The first diagnostic derived primitive is perspective. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is affect. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is potentiality. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. That is why the chapter can compare a concept theory, a machine-learning architecture, a cultural-evolution model, and an emergence theory without pretending they are the same science.

## Chapter summary

Lawrence Barsalou and Situated Conceptualization shows how a scientific theory of mind, language, culture, machines, or emergence can be heard as an idea algebra in motion. Its distinctive claim is preserved, but the formalism asks sharper questions: what composes, what resonates, what is preserved, what is suppressed, what emerges, and which horizon changes make the phenomenon appear or disappear.


# Chapter 86 — Gilles Fauconnier and Mark Turner on Conceptual Blending

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Gilles Fauconnier and Mark Turner on Conceptual Blending as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Gilles Fauconnier, pronounced approximately *zheel foe-kohn-YAY*, lived from 1944 to 2021 and worked in linguistics and cognitive science. Mark Turner, born in 1954, is an American cognitive scientist and literary scholar. Their key book is The Way We Think, published in 2002. Their famous claim is that blending is not exotic ornament but a basic operation of thought. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Gilles Fauconnier and Mark Turner generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of conceptual blending. More plainly, the mind builds small mental spaces, maps selected structure between them, and compresses them into a blended space with new meaning. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for fauconnier-turner emphasizes the same structure: The entry treats mental spaces as horizons and integration networks as the gluing of local algebras. The blend preserves selected structure, suppresses incompatible structure, and produces emergent content not present in either input. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Gilles Fauconnier and Mark Turner on Conceptual Blending, this means that the plain rule is this: the mind builds small mental spaces, maps selected structure between them, and compresses them into a blended space with new meaning. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If two input spaces share enough structure, their integration can generate stable new inferences that neither input licenses alone.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that meaning often arises in a blended space whose inferences are not available in the separate inputs. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Gilles Fauconnier and Mark Turner did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Hermeneutic Sheaf Theorem is central, because blending is local agreement plus global integration; the emergence part is also especially active. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It tells us whether the theory is mainly about gluing local meanings, about obstruction under contextual translation, or about stability across many horizons. That distinction is the algebraic signature of the theory.

## Diagnostic derived primitives

The first diagnostic derived primitive is emergence. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is perspective. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is schema. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. # Chapter 87 — George Lakoff and Mark Johnson on Conceptual Metaphor

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear George Lakoff and Mark Johnson on Conceptual Metaphor as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

George Lakoff, born in 1941, is an American linguist, and Mark Johnson, born in 1949, is an American philosopher. Their landmark book is Metaphors We Live By, published in 1980. Its most famous sentence says, in effect: our ordinary conceptual system is fundamentally metaphorical in nature. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats George Lakoff and Mark Johnson generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of conceptual metaphor. More plainly, one domain of experience, such as journey, container, balance, or war, structures another domain, such as love, argument, morality, or time. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for lakoff-johnson emphasizes the same structure: The entry reads source and target domains as horizons connected by structure-preserving translations. Metaphor carries selected relations across domains while reweighting resonance and generating meaning curvature. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For George Lakoff and Mark Johnson on Conceptual Metaphor, this means that the plain rule is this: one domain of experience, such as journey, container, balance, or war, structures another domain, such as love, argument, morality, or time. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that abstract reasoning depends on systematic cross-domain mappings grounded in embodied experience. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Indexical Pullback Theorem is most engaged, because metaphor shows how structure survives translation while meaning curvature changes inferential force. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. The first diagnostic derived primitive is schema. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is affect. # Chapter 88 — Charles Fillmore and Frame Semantics

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Charles Fillmore and Frame Semantics as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Charles Fillmore, 1929 to 2014, was an American linguist and a founder of frame semantics and construction grammar. A key text is Frame Semantics, published in 1982. His guiding thought is often expressed this way: to understand a word, you must understand the frame it evokes. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Charles Fillmore generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of frame semantics. More plainly, a word calls up a structured scene. Buy, sell, price, buyer, seller, and goods belong to one commercial event frame. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for fillmore emphasizes the same structure: The entry treats frames as horizons and lexical meaning as the local algebra living inside a frame. A word has meaning because it activates roles, expectations, and relations in a scene. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Charles Fillmore and Frame Semantics, this means that the plain rule is this: a word calls up a structured scene. Buy, sell, price, buyer, seller, and goods belong to one commercial event frame. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If two words evoke the same frame from different role positions, their meanings will resonate strongly while preserving asymmetric curvature.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that lexical meaning is frame-relative rather than an isolated pairing of word and object. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Charles Fillmore did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Hermeneutic Sheaf Theorem is central, because frame understanding requires local roles to fit together into one scene. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is differentiation. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. That is why the chapter can compare a concept theory, a machine-learning architecture, a cultural-evolution model, and an emergence theory without pretending they are the same science.

# Chapter 89 — Peter Gärdenfors and Conceptual Spaces

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. ## Biographical preamble

Peter Gärdenfors, pronounced approximately *GAIR-den-fors*, born in 1949, is a Swedish philosopher and cognitive scientist. His key book is Conceptual Spaces, published in 2000. His signature claim is that concepts can be modeled as regions in quality spaces. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Peter Gärdenfors generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of conceptual spaces. More plainly, concepts occupy regions in spaces of color, shape, taste, pitch, function, and other qualities. Similarity is nearness, and natural concepts often form convex regions. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for gardenfors emphasizes the same structure: The entry reconstructs concepts as convex regions, metric structure on horizons, and similarity as inverse distance. It gives the algebra a spatial interpretation where resonance follows nearness. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Peter Gärdenfors and Conceptual Spaces, this means that the plain rule is this: concepts occupy regions in spaces of color, shape, taste, pitch, function, and other qualities. Similarity is nearness, and natural concepts often form convex regions. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If a concept is natural, interpolated cases between good examples should usually remain inside the concept region.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that many concepts are best explained by geometry in quality dimensions rather than by definitions alone. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Peter Gärdenfors did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Profundity Theorem is engaged when a region remains stable across many quality horizons; the Indexical Pullback Theorem appears when dimensions are reweighted by context. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It tells us whether the theory is mainly about gluing local meanings, about obstruction under contextual translation, or about stability across many horizons. That distinction is the algebraic signature of the theory.

## Diagnostic derived primitives

The first diagnostic derived primitive is differentiation. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is schema. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is credence. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. That is why the chapter can compare a concept theory, a machine-learning architecture, a cultural-evolution model, and an emergence theory without pretending they are the same science.

## Chapter summary

Peter Gärdenfors and Conceptual Spaces shows how a scientific theory of mind, language, culture, machines, or emergence can be heard as an idea algebra in motion. Its distinctive claim is preserved, but the formalism asks sharper questions: what composes, what resonates, what is preserved, what is suppressed, what emerges, and which horizon changes make the phenomenon appear or disappear.


# Chapter 90 — Daniel Kahneman on System One, System Two, and Framing

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Daniel Kahneman on System One, System Two, and Framing as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Daniel Kahneman, pronounced approximately *KAH-nuh-man*, 1934 to 2024, was an Israeli American psychologist and Nobel laureate in economics. With Amos Tversky he developed prospect theory; his later synthesis is Thinking, Fast and Slow, published in 2011. A famous line says: what you see is all there is. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Daniel Kahneman generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of dual process judgment and framing. More plainly, fast intuitive judgment and slower deliberative judgment respond differently to the same situation, especially when framing changes what is salient. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for kahneman emphasizes the same structure: The entry treats System One and System Two as different processing horizons and framing effects as canonical meaning curvature. The same outcome described as survival or death can pull judgment in different directions. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If two frames preserve factual content but reverse salience, choices will diverge in predictable directions.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.
