Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that equivalent descriptions can produce different judgments because resonance is asymmetric and context-sensitive. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Daniel Kahneman did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Indexical Pullback Theorem is central, because framing is the failure of preserved content to carry identical practical force across horizons. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The first diagnostic derived primitive is meaning curvature. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is credence. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is affect. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

Paul Smolensky, born in 1955, is an American cognitive scientist known for connectionism, harmony theory, and tensor product representations. A key publication is Tensor Product Variable Binding and the Representation of Symbolic Structures in Connectionist Systems, published in 1990. The central claim is that symbolic structure can be realized in distributed neural representations. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Paul Smolensky generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## Biographical preamble

Tony Plate is a cognitive scientist and computer scientist best known for Holographic Reduced Representations, published as a 1995 paper and a 2003 book. His practical maxim is that symbols can be bound, superposed, and approximately unbound in fixed-width distributed memory. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? # Chapter 93 — Word Embeddings, word2vec, and GloVe

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Word Embeddings, word2vec, and GloVe as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Tomas Mikolov, pronounced approximately *TOH-mahsh MEE-koh-lof*, born in 1982, helped create word2vec. Jeffrey Pennington, Richard Socher, and Christopher Manning introduced GloVe in 2014. The older motto behind the field comes from J. R. Firth: you shall know a word by the company it keeps. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Tomas Mikolov, Jeffrey Pennington, Richard Socher, and Christopher Manning generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of distributional semantics. More plainly, words become positions in a learned space because contexts of use leave statistical traces. King, queen, man, and woman reveal relations through patterns of co-occurrence. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for word2vec-glove emphasizes the same structure: The entry treats co-occurrence statistics as resonance and word vectors as a learned semantic geometry. GloVe especially emphasizes ratios of co-occurrence, which the formalism reads as meaning curvature. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Word Embeddings, word2vec, and GloVe, this means that the plain rule is this: words become positions in a learned space because contexts of use leave statistical traces. King, queen, man, and woman reveal relations through patterns of co-occurrence. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If two words occur in similar contexts, their vectors will resonate; if their contextual ratios differ systematically, an interpretable relation becomes available.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that semantic relations can be recovered from distributional patterns in large corpora. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Tomas Mikolov, Jeffrey Pennington, Richard Socher, and Christopher Manning did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Profundity Theorem is engaged when relations remain stable across corpora; the Indexical Pullback Theorem appears when genre or community changes the vector field. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It tells us whether the theory is mainly about gluing local meanings, about obstruction under contextual translation, or about stability across many horizons. That distinction is the algebraic signature of the theory.

## Diagnostic derived primitives

The first diagnostic derived primitive is resonance. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is meaning curvature. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is schema. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. That is why the chapter can compare a concept theory, a machine-learning architecture, a cultural-evolution model, and an emergence theory without pretending they are the same science.

## Chapter summary

Word Embeddings, word2vec, and GloVe shows how a scientific theory of mind, language, culture, machines, or emergence can be heard as an idea algebra in motion. Its distinctive claim is preserved, but the formalism asks sharper questions: what composes, what resonates, what is preserved, what is suppressed, what emerges, and which horizon changes make the phenomenon appear or disappear.


# Chapter 94 — Transformers

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Transformers as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Ashish Vaswani and collaborators introduced the transformer architecture in the 2017 paper Attention Is All You Need. Vaswani is an Indian computer scientist, pronounced approximately *AH-sheesh vah-SWAH-nee*. The famous phrase is the title itself: attention is all you need. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Ashish Vaswani and collaborators generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of attention based sequence modeling. More plainly, each token reads the other tokens in its context window and forms a weighted representation of what matters for the next computation. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for transformers emphasizes the same structure: The entry reconstructs attention as a horizon-relative resonance computation over a context window. In-context learning becomes the temporary formation of a local algebra induced by the prompt. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Transformers, this means that the plain rule is this: each token reads the other tokens in its context window and forms a weighted representation of what matters for the next computation. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If the relevant context is present and properly attended, the model can compose distant information without step-by-step recurrent memory.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that sequence understanding can be driven by learned attention over context rather than by recurrence alone. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Ashish Vaswani and collaborators did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Hermeneutic Sheaf Theorem is central, because attention glues local token relations into a global contextual representation. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is potentiality. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. # Chapter 95 — Deep Learning Generally

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Deep Learning Generally as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Geoffrey Hinton, born in 1947, Yann LeCun, pronounced approximately *yahn luh-KUN*, born in 1960, and Yoshua Bengio, pronounced approximately *YOH-shoo-ah BEN-jee-oh*, born in 1964, helped make deep learning central to artificial intelligence. A shared lesson of their work is that useful features can be learned rather than hand engineered. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Geoffrey Hinton, Yann LeCun, and Yoshua Bengio generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of hierarchical representation learning. More plainly, layers transform raw inputs into increasingly abstract features, so edges become shapes, shapes become parts, and parts become objects or actions. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for deep-learning emphasizes the same structure: The entry treats hierarchical features as a graded algebra and depth as degree. Composition moves from lower-level features to higher-level representations, while training tunes resonance to task success. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Deep Learning Generally, this means that the plain rule is this: layers transform raw inputs into increasingly abstract features, so edges become shapes, shapes become parts, and parts become objects or actions. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If a task has hierarchical structure and enough data, deeper representations should discover intermediate features that improve generalization.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that multi-layer systems can learn useful abstractions from data by optimizing internal representations. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Geoffrey Hinton, Yann LeCun, and Yoshua Bengio did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Profundity Theorem is central, because depth is valuable when learned features remain useful across many horizons and close under further emergence. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It tells us whether the theory is mainly about gluing local meanings, about obstruction under contextual translation, or about stability across many horizons. That distinction is the algebraic signature of the theory.

The first diagnostic derived primitive is grading. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is potentiality. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is credence. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. That is why the chapter can compare a concept theory, a machine-learning architecture, a cultural-evolution model, and an emergence theory without pretending they are the same science.

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. ## Biographical preamble

Large language models are collective achievements of modern artificial intelligence research. Key publications include BERT in 2018, the GPT series beginning in 2018, and many open model families after 2023. Their practical slogan is simple: predict the next token well enough, and broad linguistic competence emerges. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? ## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of large scale language modeling. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

# Chapter 97 — Richard Dawkins and Memes

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Richard Dawkins and Memes as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Richard Dawkins, born in 1941, is a British evolutionary biologist and science writer. His key book for this chapter is The Selfish Gene, published in 1976. The famous line is: we are survival machines, robot vehicles blindly programmed to preserve the selfish molecules known as genes. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Richard Dawkins generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of memes as cultural replicators. More plainly, tunes, slogans, rituals, and techniques spread because they are copied, varied, and selected in populations of minds and media. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for dawkins emphasizes the same structure: The entry treats population horizons and memetic fitness as horizon-relative resonance. A meme succeeds when it composes well with host attention, memory, imitation, and institutions. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Richard Dawkins and Memes, this means that the plain rule is this: tunes, slogans, rituals, and techniques spread because they are copied, varied, and selected in populations of minds and media. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If a representation is easy to copy, memorable, and behavior-guiding in a population horizon, it should spread more successfully than rivals.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that cultural items can be analyzed as replicators with differential survival and transmission. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Richard Dawkins did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Profundity Theorem is engaged when memes persist across many horizons; transmission chains are also central. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It tells us whether the theory is mainly about gluing local meanings, about obstruction under contextual translation, or about stability across many horizons. That distinction is the algebraic signature of the theory.

## Diagnostic derived primitives

The first diagnostic derived primitive is transmission. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is credence. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is schema. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. That is why the chapter can compare a concept theory, a machine-learning architecture, a cultural-evolution model, and an emergence theory without pretending they are the same science.

## Chapter summary

Richard Dawkins and Memes shows how a scientific theory of mind, language, culture, machines, or emergence can be heard as an idea algebra in motion. Its distinctive claim is preserved, but the formalism asks sharper questions: what composes, what resonates, what is preserved, what is suppressed, what emerges, and which horizon changes make the phenomenon appear or disappear.


# Chapter 98 — Dan Sperber and the Epidemiology of Representations

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Dan Sperber and the Epidemiology of Representations as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Dan Sperber, pronounced approximately *dahn SPEHR-bair*, born in 1942, is a French anthropologist and cognitive scientist. His key book is Explaining Culture, published in 1996. His guiding claim is that culture is not copied like a substance; it is reconstructed through chains of mental and public representations. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Dan Sperber generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of epidemiology of representations. More plainly, ideas spread through transformations between what is in a mind and what is in the public environment, such as speech, gesture, text, and ritual. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for sperber emphasizes the same structure: The entry treats public and mental representations as different horizons connected by transmission morphisms. Stability comes not from perfect copying but from attractors that make reconstructions converge. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Dan Sperber and the Epidemiology of Representations, this means that the plain rule is this: ideas spread through transformations between what is in a mind and what is in the public environment, such as speech, gesture, text, and ritual. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If many people reconstruct a representation toward the same attractor, the culture can remain stable despite noisy transmission.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that cultural stability depends on cognitive attractors and public transmission, not exact replication alone. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. ## Biographical preamble

Joseph Henrich, born in 1968, is an American anthropologist and evolutionary theorist. Key books include The Secret of Our Success, published in 2015, and The WEIRDest People in the World, published in 2020. A recurring claim is that human intelligence is deeply collective and cultural. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Joseph Henrich generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of cumulative culture. More plainly, skills and institutions accumulate because learners inherit practices they could not invent alone, then slightly improve or recombine them. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

# Chapter 100 — Robert Boyd and Peter Richerson on Conformist and Prestige Bias

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Robert Boyd and Peter Richerson on Conformist and Prestige Bias as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Robert Boyd, born in 1948, and Peter Richerson, born in 1943, are American evolutionary social scientists. Their key book is Culture and the Evolutionary Process, published in 1985. Their central lesson is that social learning is biased, and those biases can be adaptive. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Robert Boyd and Peter Richerson generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of biased cultural transmission. More plainly, people copy common behaviors, prestigious models, successful models, and familiar in-group practices rather than sampling culture at random. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for boyd-richerson emphasizes the same structure: The entry treats conformist and prestige biases as preference structures on transmission morphisms. Cultural evolution is shaped by how horizons weight possible teachers and variants. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Robert Boyd and Peter Richerson on Conformist and Prestige Bias, this means that the plain rule is this: people copy common behaviors, prestigious models, successful models, and familiar in-group practices rather than sampling culture at random. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that social learning biases predict which cultural variants spread under uncertainty. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. ## Biographical preamble

Alex Mesoudi, pronounced approximately *meh-SOO-dee*, born in 1978, is a British cultural evolution researcher. His key book is Cultural Evolution, published in 2011. His guiding claim is that cultural change can be studied with the same population logic that transformed biology, while respecting cultural differences. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? ## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of Darwinian cultural evolution. More plainly, variation, inheritance, and selection operate on cultural representations, practices, and artifacts, though cultural inheritance is often horizontal and reconstructive. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Alex Mesoudi did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Profundity Theorem is engaged when variants stabilize across generations; the Hermeneutic Sheaf Theorem appears in shared cultural reconstruction. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It tells us whether the theory is mainly about gluing local meanings, about obstruction under contextual translation, or about stability across many horizons. That distinction is the algebraic signature of the theory.

## Diagnostic derived primitives

The first diagnostic derived primitive is transmission. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is temporality. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is credence. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. That is why the chapter can compare a concept theory, a machine-learning architecture, a cultural-evolution model, and an emergence theory without pretending they are the same science.

## Chapter summary

Alex Mesoudi and Cultural Evolution as Darwinian shows how a scientific theory of mind, language, culture, machines, or emergence can be heard as an idea algebra in motion. Its distinctive claim is preserved, but the formalism asks sharper questions: what composes, what resonates, what is preserved, what is suppressed, what emerges, and which horizon changes make the phenomenon appear or disappear.


# Chapter 102 — Susan Blackmore and the Meme Machine

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear Susan Blackmore and the Meme Machine as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

Susan Blackmore, born in 1951, is a British psychologist and writer on consciousness and memetics. Her key book is The Meme Machine, published in 1999. Her memorable claim is that humans are not merely gene machines but meme machines, shaped by imitation. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Susan Blackmore generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of memetic imitation. More plainly, imitation creates a second replicator system in which cultural items compete for attention, memory, and reproduction through human behavior. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for blackmore emphasizes the same structure: The entry treats imitation as a primitive transmission morphism and raises the question of memetic agency. Memes are modeled by their capacity to recruit copying machinery. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For Susan Blackmore and the Meme Machine, this means that the plain rule is this: imitation creates a second replicator system in which cultural items compete for attention, memory, and reproduction through human behavior. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If an item improves its own chance of being imitated, it can spread even when it does not improve host welfare.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that imitation enables memes to form a distinct evolutionary dynamic with humans as copying hosts. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. Susan Blackmore did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Profundity Theorem is engaged by durable meme complexes, while the Indexical Pullback Theorem explains host-dependent distortions. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It tells us whether the theory is mainly about gluing local meanings, about obstruction under contextual translation, or about stability across many horizons. That distinction is the algebraic signature of the theory.

## Diagnostic derived primitives

The first diagnostic derived primitive is transmission. It names the way the theory turns raw representation into a usable role inside a horizon. The second is affect. It marks the local organization that lets the representation guide expectation, action, or explanation. The third is reflexion. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

These primitives are derived, not added. They come from composition, resonance, the three parts of combination, grading, and horizon variation. That is why the chapter can compare a concept theory, a machine-learning architecture, a cultural-evolution model, and an emergence theory without pretending they are the same science.

## Chapter summary

Its distinctive claim is preserved, but the formalism asks sharper questions: what composes, what resonates, what is preserved, what is suppressed, what emerges, and which horizon changes make the phenomenon appear or disappear.

Michael Tomasello, pronounced approximately *toh-mah-ZEL-oh*, born in 1950, is an American developmental and comparative psychologist. Key books include The Cultural Origins of Human Cognition, published in 1999, and A Natural History of Human Thinking, published in 2014. His central claim is that shared intentionality is a foundation of uniquely human cognition. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats Michael Tomasello generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

# Chapter 104 — John Stuart Mill and Heteropathic Causation

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another. Every composition has a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part. The horizon category is the field of contexts in which meaning is evaluated, and the horizon functor — the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi — assigns a local idea algebra to each context. With those reminders in place, we can hear John Stuart Mill and Heteropathic Causation as both a scientific theory and a precise sub-instance of idea theory.

## Biographical preamble

John Stuart Mill, 1806 to 1873, was a British philosopher, economist, and logician. His System of Logic, published in 1843, contains the classic discussion of heteropathic effects. A useful line from Mill says that the laws of the phenomena of society are and can be nothing but the laws of the actions and passions of human beings united together. For our purposes the important point is not only the historical name, but the problem on the desk: how can minds, machines, or cultures handle meanings that are structured, flexible, transmissible, and sometimes surprising? The chapter treats John Stuart Mill generously, as a builder of a real scientific voice, and then asks what happens when that voice is reconstructed inside the algebra of ideas.

## The theory in its own scientific voice

In its own voice, the theory speaks in the language of heteropathic causation. More plainly, some causes combine by simple addition, while others produce effects with new laws, as chemical combination differs from mechanical mixture. The science begins by refusing to treat ideas as inert labels. It asks what a thinker, learner, model, or population actually does with a representation. The answer is not merely that the representation is present. It has a role. It sorts cases, guides attention, licenses inferences, enables prediction, supports memory, recruits action, or travels through a community.

The zoo entry for mill emphasizes the same structure: The entry treats Mill as a classical statement of emergence and reads heteropathic causation through the emergence cocycle. Composition sometimes yields more than preserved inputs. This matters because the original theory is empirical. It is answerable to reaction times, developmental patterns, neural architectures, linguistic behavior, transmission rates, or explanatory success. It is not enough to say that the theory is elegant. It must organize observations better than its rivals, and it must tell us where breakdowns will occur.

## The theory as a sub-instance of the idea algebra

In idea-algebra terms, the objects are the relevant representations: concepts, words, frames, vectors, practices, stances, models, or emergent patterns. Composition is the operation by which two such representations are used together. The resonance pairing measures the attraction, compatibility, contrast, or retrieval strength between them. The preservation part is what survives the combination. The suppression part is what gets screened off, weakened, inhibited, or made irrelevant. The emergence part is what becomes available only after the combination has occurred.

For John Stuart Mill and Heteropathic Causation, this means that the plain rule is this: some causes combine by simple addition, while others produce effects with new laws, as chemical combination differs from mechanical mixture. That rule becomes a local rule inside a horizon. A horizon might be a task, a body state, a discourse frame, a population, a training corpus, a developmental relation, a level of organization, or a first-person point of view. The horizon functor then says that the whole algebra changes with context, not merely one score or one label. Resonance, composition, emergence, grading, and even what counts as a stable idea are all horizon-relative.

## Central empirical claim as theorem

*If the joint effect cannot be predicted by adding separate effects, the composition contains a genuine emergence part.*

This theorem rules out a flat interpretation of the theory. The central claim is not that a certain word, concept, system, or culture has one fixed content independent of use. It predicts that the content will be stable only where the relevant horizon structure is stable. When the horizon changes, the theory expects a principled change in resonance, preservation, suppression, or emergence. The empirical work then asks whether those changes can be observed.

Careful semi-formal restatement: within the local idea algebra assigned by the horizon functor, take the representations named by the theory as elements of the idea domain. Compose them according to the theory-specific operation. Evaluate their resonance pairing, identify the preservation part, identify the suppression part, and identify the emergence part. The theorem says that some combinations produce effects whose laws are not mere sums of component laws. If this condition fails, the theory loses its explanatory force in that horizon; if it holds across many horizons, the theory earns trans-horizonal stability.

## What the formalism adds

The formalism adds three gains. First, it separates similarity from resonance. Two things can look alike while calling up different actions, and two things can look different while playing the same role. Second, it forces the theory to say what is preserved, what is suppressed, and what genuinely emerges when representations combine. Third, it makes context explicit through horizons rather than leaving context as a loose qualification.

This yields testable predictions. If the account is right, changes of horizon should produce patterned changes rather than noise. A task shift, a new frame, a new social model, a new corpus, a developmental scaffold, or a new level of organization should alter the resonance field in a way that can be measured. The formalism also sharpens failure cases. When local meanings cannot be glued into a coherent whole, we should see conflict, hallucination, conceptual change, failed translation, or unstable transmission.

## Where the formalism diverges

The formalism also diverges from the original theory. It is less interested in the historical vocabulary of the theory and more interested in the structural commitments that make the theory work. It may treat a psychological process, a linguistic frame, a neural architecture, and a cultural transmission chain as comparable if they share the same algebraic profile. That comparison is powerful, but it can flatten differences the original researcher cared about.

So the reconstruction should not be mistaken for replacement. John Stuart Mill did not necessarily claim to be building an idea algebra. The algebra says: here is the minimal structure that would make the theory true, portable, and testable. Where the original theory is richer in mechanism, experiment, or historical detail, the formalism abstracts. Where the original theory is loose about context, novelty, or levels, the formalism demands precision.

## Horizon structure and the engaged theorem

The Profundity Theorem is less central than the emergence theorem, but the Indexical Pullback Theorem appears when lower-level descriptions fail to carry upper-level force. In practical terms, imagine a diagram described in words. At the left is a local horizon, such as a task, conversation, body state, training context, population, or level of organization. At the right is another horizon. Between them is a translation of context. Under each horizon sits a full local algebra of ideas. The question is whether meaning data travels cleanly, whether it must be glued from partial overlaps, or whether it becomes profound by remaining stable across many such moves.

For this chapter, the most engaged horizon-functor theorem is therefore the one named above. It identifies the pressure point where the theory becomes empirically visible, because changes in this primitive should change judgments, behavior, transmission, or prediction.

# Chapter 105 — C. D. Broad and Emergent Properties

This chapter uses the same operating vocabulary throughout. An idea can compose with another idea. The resonance pairing measures how strongly one idea calls up, supports, or resists another.
