An idea can be stable across every horizon we can reach and still be dependent on the whole practice of proof, notation, training, and abstraction. Conversely, an idea may be genuinely independent in a metaphysical sense that the framework can only approximate by support and stability. Here the theory is a grammar for the debate, not a verdict.

## Moral content

Moral ideas compose, resonate, transmit, and curve. Justice, mercy, dignity, harm, desert, loyalty, and freedom all behave like ideas in the algebra. They preserve some content across cultures, suppress other content, and generate new content when they meet institutions and technologies. The theory can describe moral disagreement with unusual precision.

It can also derive signed valence. A moral idea may attract or repel a self within a horizon. It can derive credence, so that moral uncertainty can be modeled. It can use the hermeneutic sheaf theorem to ask whether local moral readings agree on overlaps. It can use the profundity theorem to ask whether a moral claim has broad support and faithful transmission.

Still, none of that proves moral truth. A cruel norm may be stable within a large horizon. A liberating norm may begin as a fragile local section. Broad support is not the same as rightness. Strong valence is not the same as obligation. Agreement on overlaps is not the same as justice. The theory can show how moral content travels and stabilizes. It cannot, by itself, supply the authority of the moral ought.

## Risks internal to the framework

The first internal risk is over-unification. A framework that can redescribe many theories may become too permissive. If every position can be translated into the vocabulary, the translation may hide more than it reveals. The cure is prediction and contrast. A reconstruction should say what would count against it.

The second risk is false precision. Words such as horizon, resonance, and emergence are disciplined by the formalism, but in prose they can become loose again. The listener should resist decorative use. Ask what composes, what resonates, what is preserved, what is suppressed, what emerges, and which horizon is active.

The third risk is historical flattening. Plato is not Hegel. Hegel is not Saussure. Saussure is not Rosch. The theory provides a common grammar, not a claim that the thinkers secretly meant the same thing. The differences matter, especially where their horizon categories, favored primitives, and standards of evidence diverge.

## What self-criticism preserves

A theory that admits limits does not weaken itself. It becomes more usable. The framework is strongest where ideas combine, travel, shift context, create novelty, and stabilize across communities. It is less decisive where the question concerns raw phenomenal feel, ultimate mathematical being, or moral authority. That boundary is not a failure. It is a map of the next work.

## The danger of beautiful vocabulary

A final limitation is rhetorical. The vocabulary of horizons, curvature, emergence, and resonance is attractive. Attractive vocabulary can become a substitute for explanation. The listener should be suspicious whenever the terms are used without a case. If someone says that a political idea has meaning curvature, ask which two directions of resonance differ. If someone says a tradition has profundity, ask across which horizons it remains stable. If someone says a theory creates emergence, ask what prior closure it exceeds.

The framework is meant to discipline imagination, not decorate it. Its best use is slow: choose the horizon, identify the composition, name the preserved content, name the suppressed content, name the emergent content, and then ask whether the resulting reading travels. Where that cannot be done, silence may be more honest than formal language.

## Where pluralism remains

The theory also leaves room for pluralism about method. A phenomenologist may use it to describe lived horizons. A logician may use it to inspect formal equivalence. A social theorist may use it to trace institutions. A cognitive scientist may use it to build experiments. These uses need not collapse into one master discipline. The algebra is a meeting language, not a command language. It coordinates inquiry without erasing the local standards that make each field responsible.

## Chapter summary

Idea Theory clarifies consciousness, mathematics, and moral content without pretending to finish them. Its limits are not blank spaces but sharpened questions: what remains after reflexion, what stability implies about mathematical being, and whether moral truth is more than trans-horizonal force.


# Chapter 153 — A Final Letter to the Listener

Before we close, let us recall the small machine we have been carrying: ideas compose, resonate, preserve, suppress, and generate emergence; they have degrees, curvature, histories of transmission, and horizons of intelligibility. The horizon functor, the rule mathematicians label with the Greek letter xi, has let us say that context is not an afterthought. It is where the whole algebra lives.

## A word of thanks

Dear listener, thank you for staying with a difficult book. You have heard ancient metaphysics, medieval theology, German idealism, phenomenology, structuralism, analytic philosophy, cognitive science, artificial intelligence, cultural evolution, economics, game theory, and social theory threaded through one vocabulary. That is a lot to ask of the ear. If parts blurred, that is not failure. A book like this is meant to be returned to.

Perhaps one phrase stayed with you: no view from nowhere. Perhaps another: preservation, suppression, emergence. Perhaps another: understanding is local readings that glue. A theory is alive when it leaves you with handles like these, phrases you can carry into a seminar, a lab meeting, a design session, a political argument, or a quiet walk.

## How to use what remains

The invitation is simple. When you meet an idea, do not ask only whether you agree. Ask what horizon makes it intelligible. Ask what it composes with. Ask what it preserves from older ideas, what it suppresses, and what it makes newly possible. Ask where its resonance is symmetric and where its meaning curves. Ask whether it travels or only sounds universal from inside one room.

This habit is generous. It lets you hear why a position was attractive before you criticize it. It is also exacting. It prevents you from mistaking a local slogan for a global truth. The best use of the framework is not winning arguments. It is locating them.

## An open project

The formal Lean verification is intentionally not narrated in this audiobook. For those interested in the machine-checked proofs, the project website gives the technical path. But the spirit of verification belongs here too. To verify an idea is to ask whether its parts still hold when the context changes.

If you are a philosopher, test the reconstructions. If you are a cognitive scientist, test the predictions. If you are an economist, examine the gluing conditions behind markets and institutions. If you are a programmer or mathematician, inspect the formalization. If you are simply a patient listener, use the vocabulary wherever ideas matter.

## Goodbye for now

A final book should not pretend to have the final word. Ideas do not end that way. They pass from voice to voice, from horizon to horizon, preserving something, losing something, and making something new. If this book has done its work, it has become part of your own algebra now, available for recomposition.

Thank you for listening. May your next conversation have more patience, your next disagreement more structure, and your next new idea enough room to emerge.

## One last practice

Try this once after the book ends. Take an idea you care about, perhaps justice, intelligence, friendship, faith, market, proof, or home. Say what it means in your present horizon. Then imagine it in another person's horizon, not as a caricature but as a real home base. Notice what survives, what is suppressed, and what becomes newly possible. If the exercise makes you slower and kinder without making you less exact, the theory has already done practical work.

## Keeping the conversation open

If you disagree with the book, you are already inside its best future. State the horizon from which you object. Name the part of the machinery that fails. Offer a better reconstruction. The project grows not by collecting agreement, but by making disagreement precise enough to transmit.

## Chapter summary

The closing invitation is to use Idea Theory as a generous and exacting habit: locate horizons, trace composition, test translation, and join the project as a co-investigator rather than a passive receiver.


# Appendix A — A Short Biographical Dictionary of the One Hundred and Nineteen Thinkers

This appendix is a listening dictionary. Each entry gives a pronunciation gloss, life span, intellectual location, and a compact Idea Theory handle. The entries are grouped by the three large zoos: philosophy, cognitive science and artificial intelligence, and the social sciences with game theory.

## Philosophy zoo: fifty-five thinkers

### Heraclitus

Heraclitus, pronounced approximately *heh-RAK-li-tus*, about 535 to about 475 before the common era, worked in early Greek philosophy. He wrote in fragments about fire, flux, conflict, and the logos that orders change. His famous river image teaches that identity can persist only through transformation.

In Idea Theory, he is the philosopher of temporal horizons and stable pattern inside change. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Parmenides

Parmenides, pronounced approximately *par-MEN-i-deez*, born about 515 before the common era, worked in early Greek philosophy. He argued that what is truly real is one, ungenerated, and unchanging. The way of truth and the way of opinion became a founding split in metaphysics.

In Idea Theory, he marks the temptation to collapse all horizons into one rigid support. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Plato

Plato, pronounced approximately *PLAY-toh*, about 428 to 348 before the common era, worked in Greek philosophy. He made the Forms the stable objects of knowledge and treated ordinary things as partial participants in them. His dialogues turn philosophy into dramatic inquiry.

In Idea Theory, the Forms become rigid global sections and the divided line becomes a graded horizon structure. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Aristotle

Aristotle, pronounced approximately *AIR-ih-stot-ul*, 384 to 322 before the common era, worked in Greek philosophy and science. He analyzed substance, cause, logic, virtue, and living form with unmatched range. His distinction between potentiality and actuality shaped later metaphysics.

In Idea Theory, potentiality is derived from future refinements of resonance, while substance names a stable compositional center. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Plotinus

Plotinus, pronounced approximately *ploh-TY-nus*, about 204 to 270, worked in Neoplatonism. He described reality as emanating from the One through Intellect and Soul. His vision is hierarchical, contemplative, and intensely metaphysical.

In Idea Theory, emanation is heard as graded descent through horizons whose unity is approached as a limit. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Augustine of Hippo

Augustine of Hippo, pronounced approximately *aw-GUS-tin of HIP-oh*, 354 to 430, worked in Christian philosophy. He explored memory, time, will, sin, and divine illumination. In the Confessions he found time stretched through the soul.

In Idea Theory, temporality becomes a derived structure of lived horizons, especially memory, attention, and expectation. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Avicenna

Avicenna, pronounced approximately *av-ih-SEN-uh*, 980 to 1037, worked in Islamic philosophy. Also known as Ibn Sina, he distinguished essence from existence and built a powerful synthesis of Aristotle and Neoplatonism. His influence crossed Arabic, Persian, Jewish, and Latin worlds.

In Idea Theory, essence is what a section can be considered to be, while existence concerns its actual support in a horizon. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Maimonides

Maimonides, pronounced approximately *my-MON-ih-deez*, 1138 to 1204, worked in Jewish philosophy. Moses Maimonides joined law, medicine, Aristotle, and negative theology. He taught that divine language often works by saying what God is not.

In Idea Theory, negative theology becomes a disciplined refusal to force a local name into a global section. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Aquinas

Aquinas, pronounced approximately *uh-KWY-nus*, 1225 to 1274, worked in Christian scholastic philosophy. Thomas Aquinas joined Aristotelian philosophy with Christian doctrine. His analogical account of being allowed one word to apply across very different orders.

In Idea Theory, analogy is a horizon translation that preserves enough resonance without pretending to preserve everything. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Duns Scotus

Duns Scotus, pronounced approximately *dunz SKOH-tus*, about 1266 to 1308, worked in medieval scholastic philosophy. John Duns Scotus defended the univocity of being and the thisness of individuals. He sharpened medieval metaphysics with extraordinary technical care.

In Idea Theory, univocity is the demand that one stable section span the horizons where being is spoken. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Ockham

Ockham, pronounced approximately *OK-um*, about 1287 to 1347, worked in late medieval philosophy. William of Ockham defended nominalism and a disciplined refusal of needless entities. His razor became a symbol of ontological economy.

In Idea Theory, nominalism denies that universals must be global sections over and above local signs and uses. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Descartes

Descartes, pronounced approximately *day-KART*, 1596 to 1650, worked in early modern philosophy. Rene Descartes sought certainty through methodic doubt. The cogito made self-thinking a foundation for knowledge.

In Idea Theory, the cogito becomes reflexion, a fixed point where the algebra of thought turns back on itself. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Spinoza

Spinoza, pronounced approximately *spi-NOH-zuh*, 1632 to 1677, worked in early modern rationalism. Baruch Spinoza described one substance with infinite attributes and treated striving as central to finite beings. His Ethics joins geometry and liberation.

In Idea Theory, conatus is the affective gradient by which an agent persists and increases its power of acting. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Locke

Locke, pronounced approximately *lok*, 1632 to 1704, worked in British empiricism. John Locke analyzed ideas as materials of understanding and distinguished simple from complex ideas. He also shaped liberal political thought.

In Idea Theory, simple ideas are generators, complex ideas are compositions, and primary qualities aspire to broader horizon support. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Leibniz

Leibniz, pronounced approximately *LIBE-nits*, 1646 to 1716, worked in rationalist philosophy. Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz imagined monads, possible worlds, and pre-established harmony. He combined metaphysics, mathematics, and logic.

In Idea Theory, monads are local perspectives, while harmony is the claim that their local readings can still glue. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Berkeley

Berkeley, pronounced approximately *BARK-lee*, 1685 to 1753, worked in empiricist philosophy. George Berkeley argued that to be is to be perceived. He denied matter as a mind-independent substratum.

In Idea Theory, his idealism treats ideas as sections over perceiving perspectives, with God as the maximal stabilizing horizon. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Hume

Hume, pronounced approximately *hyoom*, 1711 to 1776, worked in Scottish empiricism. David Hume explained mind through impressions, ideas, habit, and association. Causation became a pattern of expectation rather than a directly perceived necessity.

In Idea Theory, resonance carries resemblance, contiguity, and habitual expectation, while causation becomes stabilized transmission. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Kant

Kant, pronounced approximately *kahnt*, 1724 to 1804, worked in critical philosophy. Immanuel Kant argued that experience is shaped by categories, forms of intuition, and the unity of apperception. He transformed metaphysics into critique.

In Idea Theory, the categories are schema constraints and the subject is a perspective that conditions possible horizons. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Fichte

Fichte, pronounced approximately *FIKH-tuh*, 1762 to 1814, worked in German idealism. Johann Gottlieb Fichte made the self-positing I the starting point of philosophy. Freedom and activity replace passive representation.

In Idea Theory, the self-positing I is reflexion as a productive fixed point rather than a mere mirror. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Schelling

Schelling, pronounced approximately *SHEL-ing*, 1775 to 1854, worked in German idealism. Friedrich Schelling treated nature and spirit as related expressions of one absolute process. His work moved through nature philosophy, art, and freedom.

In Idea Theory, nature and mind become connected horizons rather than separate substances. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Hegel

Hegel, pronounced approximately *HAY-gul*, 1770 to 1831, worked in German idealism. Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel made contradiction, development, and sublation central. His logic and phenomenology describe thought passing through its own shapes.

In Idea Theory, preservation, suppression, and emergence give formal discipline to sublation. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Schopenhauer

Schopenhauer, pronounced approximately *SHOH-pen-how-er*, 1788 to 1860, worked in post-Kantian philosophy. Arthur Schopenhauer saw the world as representation and will. Desire, suffering, art, and compassion organize his pessimistic metaphysics.

In Idea Theory, will is heard as affective drive beneath representation, a gradient pressing through horizons. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Marx

Marx, pronounced approximately *marks*, 1818 to 1883, worked in social and political philosophy. Karl Marx analyzed capitalism through labor, value, class struggle, ideology, and historical transformation. He turned Hegel toward material life.

In Idea Theory, modes of production are paradigm components, and ideology is a failed translation from material to official horizons. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Kierkegaard

Kierkegaard, pronounced approximately *KEER-kuh-gard*, 1813 to 1855, worked in existential philosophy. Soren Kierkegaard wrote under many pseudonyms about faith, anxiety, despair, and inwardness. He resisted system by staging perspectives.

In Idea Theory, the leap of faith is a horizon crossing without a continuous inferential path. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Nietzsche

Nietzsche, pronounced approximately *NEE-chuh*, 1844 to 1900, worked in genealogy and existential philosophy. Friedrich Nietzsche attacked idols, moral complacency, and metaphysical comfort. Perspectivism, will to power, and genealogy mark his style.

In Idea Theory, perspectivism becomes the refusal of a privileged horizon and genealogy becomes transmission analysis. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Husserl

Husserl, pronounced approximately *HUSS-url*, 1859 to 1938, worked in phenomenology. Edmund Husserl founded phenomenology by returning to the structures of experience. Intentionality and the constitution of objects are central.

In Idea Theory, intentionality is the resonance profile of an idea across horizons of appearance. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Heidegger

Heidegger, pronounced approximately *HY-deg-er*, 1889 to 1976, worked in phenomenology and ontology. Martin Heidegger asked the question of Being through Dasein, care, temporality, and worldhood. His language is difficult but influential.

In Idea Theory, Dasein is perspective, care is affective orientation, and Being names trans-horizonal disclosedness. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Merleau-Ponty

Merleau-Ponty, pronounced approximately *mair-LOH pont-TEE*, 1908 to 1961, worked in French phenomenology. Maurice Merleau-Ponty centered embodiment, perception, and the lived body. He rejected a detached spectator model of mind.

In Idea Theory, the body supplies the home horizon from which perception and action become intelligible. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Gadamer

Gadamer, pronounced approximately *GAH-duh-mer*, 1900 to 2002, worked in hermeneutics. Hans-Georg Gadamer described understanding as a fusion of horizons shaped by history, language, and tradition. Interpretation is dialogical.

In Idea Theory, his fusion of horizons is the clearest philosophical image of sheaf-like gluing. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Ricoeur

Ricoeur, pronounced approximately *rih-KUR*, 1913 to 2005, worked in hermeneutics. Paul Ricoeur worked on metaphor, narrative, symbol, memory, and selfhood. He joined suspicion with restoration of meaning.

In Idea Theory, narrative identity is a trans-horizonal section that holds a self together across episodes. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Saussure

Saussure, pronounced approximately *soh-SOOR*, 1857 to 1913, worked in linguistics and structuralism. Ferdinand de Saussure taught that signs gain value through differences within a system. Language is structure, not a list of names.

In Idea Theory, differentiation is derived from resonant alternatives that a sign suppresses. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Derrida

Derrida, pronounced approximately *deh-ree-DAH*, 1930 to 2004, worked in post-structuralism. Jacques Derrida developed deconstruction, trace, supplement, and differance. He showed how texts exceed stable presence.

In Idea Theory, differance is a temporal and differential instability in the suppression and preservation of meaning. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Foucault

Foucault, pronounced approximately *foo-KOH*, 1926 to 1984, worked in history of thought. Michel Foucault studied discourse, power, discipline, sexuality, and epistemes. He tracked the rules that make statements possible.

In Idea Theory, an episteme is a paradigm component and an epistemic break is a horizon shift. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Deleuze

Deleuze, pronounced approximately *duh-LOOZ*, 1925 to 1995, worked in French philosophy. Gilles Deleuze wrote on difference, repetition, desire, cinema, and immanence. With Guattari he made concepts mobile and inventive.

In Idea Theory, concepts are singular compositions on a plane of immanence, rich in emergence. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Bakhtin

Bakhtin, pronounced approximately *bahk-TEEN*, 1895 to 1975, worked in Russian literary theory. Mikhail Bakhtin emphasized dialogue, heteroglossia, carnival, and the many-voiced novel. Meaning is never simply single-voiced.

In Idea Theory, heteroglossia is the audible plurality of horizons inside language. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Peirce

Peirce, pronounced approximately *purse*, 1839 to 1914, worked in American pragmatism and semiotics. Charles Sanders Peirce built a triadic theory of signs, objects, and interpretants. He also developed abduction and fallibilism.

In Idea Theory, the sign relation is a structured horizon translation and abduction searches for a generating section. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### James

James, pronounced approximately *jaymz*, 1842 to 1910, worked in American pragmatism. William James wrote on psychology, religion, truth, and the stream of consciousness. Pragmatism tied meaning to consequences in experience.

In Idea Theory, truth is a section that survives inquiry and revision across lived horizons. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Dewey

Dewey, pronounced approximately *DOO-ee*, 1859 to 1952, worked in American pragmatism. John Dewey treated inquiry, democracy, education, and experience as continuous practical processes. Knowledge grows through problem solving.

In Idea Theory, inquiry is controlled horizon shifting that repairs blocked composition. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Frege

Frege, pronounced approximately *FRAY-guh*, 1848 to 1925, worked in logic and analytic philosophy. Gottlob Frege distinguished sense and reference and helped found modern logic. His context principle shaped analytic philosophy.

In Idea Theory, sense is the route of presentation across horizons, while reference seeks stable extension. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Russell

Russell, pronounced approximately *RUSS-ul*, 1872 to 1970, worked in analytic philosophy. Bertrand Russell worked on logic, descriptions, atomism, mathematics, and public reason. He pursued clarity with enormous influence.

In Idea Theory, descriptions separate local identifying structure from rigid reference. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Wittgenstein

Wittgenstein, pronounced approximately *VIT-gen-shtine*, 1889 to 1951, worked in analytic philosophy. Ludwig Wittgenstein first sought the logical form of language and later described meaning as use in language games. His two periods shaped a century.

In Idea Theory, the early work seeks global picturing, while the later work makes horizons local and practical. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Quine

Quine, pronounced approximately *kwine*, 1908 to 2000, worked in analytic philosophy. Willard Van Orman Quine challenged analyticity, reductionism, and determinate translation. His web of belief made knowledge holistic.

In Idea Theory, translation indeterminacy becomes an indexical obstruction across horizons. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Fodor

Fodor, pronounced approximately *FOH-der*, 1935 to 2017, worked in philosophy of mind. Jerry Fodor defended a language of thought and modularity of mind. He argued for inner symbolic structure.

In Idea Theory, the language of thought is the proposal of a privileged generating algebra. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. ### Brandom

Brandom, pronounced approximately *BRAN-dum*, born 1950, worked in analytic pragmatism. Robert Brandom develops inferentialism, scorekeeping, and recognition as central to meaning. Meaning is what follows from use.

In Idea Theory, the emergence part, what a composition licenses, becomes the heart of semantic content. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Sellars

Sellars, pronounced approximately *SEL-erz*, 1912 to 1989, worked in analytic philosophy. Wilfrid Sellars attacked the myth of the given and described the space of reasons. He joined Kantian themes to analytic rigor.

In Idea Theory, no raw ground horizon is allowed; knowledge lives in norm-governed translation. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Davidson

Davidson, pronounced approximately *DAY-vid-sun*, 1917 to 2003, worked in philosophy of language. Donald Davidson developed radical interpretation, triangulation, and truth-conditional semantics. Charity became a condition of interpretation.

In Idea Theory, speaker, interpreter, and world form a span of horizons whose readings must glue. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Kripke

Kripke, pronounced approximately *KRIP-kee*, 1940 to 2022, worked in logic and philosophy of language. Saul Kripke transformed modal logic, naming, necessity, and rule-following debates. Rigid designation is his most famous semantic tool.

In Idea Theory, rigid reference is a global section whose extension remains singleton across horizons. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Nagarjuna

Nagarjuna, pronounced approximately *nah-GAR-joo-nah*, about 150 to about 250, worked in Madhyamaka Buddhist philosophy. Nagarjuna taught emptiness and dependent origination by dismantling claims to inherent existence. His arguments are subtle and radical.

In Idea Theory, emptiness becomes reflexion applied to the absence of fixed independent support. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Confucius, pronounced approximately *kun-FYOO-shus*, 551 to 479 before the common era, worked in classical Chinese philosophy. Confucius taught ritual, humaneness, learning, and the rectification of names. Ethical life is cultivated in relation.

Zhuangzi, pronounced approximately *jwahng-DZUH*, about 369 to 286 before the common era, worked in Daoist philosophy. Zhuangzi used stories, jokes, and reversals to unsettle fixed perspectives. The butterfly dream remains his emblem.

### Laozi

Laozi, pronounced approximately *LOW-dzuh*, traditionally sixth century before the common era, worked in Daoist tradition. Laozi is the legendary author associated with the Dao De Jing. The text praises the way, non-forcing action, and supple reversal.

In Idea Theory, the Dao names a limit of articulation, where composition works best by not over-fixing the horizon. ### Dignaga

Dignaga, pronounced approximately *dig-NAH-guh*, about 480 to 540, worked in Buddhist logic. Dignaga reshaped Indian epistemology through perception, inference, and exclusion. His apoha theory treats meaning through what a term excludes.

In Idea Theory, meaning through exclusion is close to differentiation and suppression among resonant alternatives. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Al-Farabi, pronounced approximately *al fah-RAH-bee*, about 872 to 950, worked in Islamic philosophy. Al-Farabi joined Plato and Aristotle to political philosophy, logic, and metaphysics. He treated the virtuous city as ordered by intellect.

In Idea Theory, the virtuous city is a social horizon ordered by shared intelligible forms. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ### Boethius

Boethius, pronounced approximately *boh-EE-thee-us*, about 480 to 524, worked in late antique philosophy. Boethius transmitted Greek logic to the Latin Middle Ages and wrote The Consolation of Philosophy in prison. Fortune and providence frame his thought.

In Idea Theory, translation across civilizations is itself a transmission chain with enormous horizon support. Simone de Beauvoir, pronounced approximately *see-MOHN duh boh-VWAHR*, 1908 to 1986, worked in existential and feminist philosophy. She analyzed freedom, ambiguity, embodiment, and the social making of womanhood. The Second Sex changed philosophy and politics.

In Idea Theory, gender appears as a social horizon whose categories can be exposed, resisted, and recomposed. ## Cognitive science and artificial intelligence zoo: thirty-two thinkers

### Eleanor Rosch

Eleanor Rosch, pronounced approximately *EL-uh-nor rosh*, born 1938, worked in cognitive psychology. She showed that categories have prototypes and typicality gradients rather than only crisp definitions. Her experiments changed the study of concepts.

In Idea Theory, prototype effects become horizon-relative resonance profiles. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Gregory Murphy, pronounced approximately *GREG-uh-ree MUR-fee*, born 1949, worked in psychology of concepts. He developed the theory-theory of concepts and showed how categories depend on explanatory knowledge. Concepts are embedded in background theories.

In Idea Theory, folk theories are local horizons that shape resonance and inference. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. Lawrence Barsalou, pronounced approximately *LAW-rens BAR-suh-loo*, born 1951, worked in cognitive science. He developed situated conceptualization and perceptual symbol systems. Concepts are simulated in bodily and modal contexts.

In Idea Theory, simulation is a horizon-bound recomposition of perceptual and action structure. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Gilles Fauconnier, pronounced approximately *zheel foh-koh-NYAY*, 1944 to 2021, worked in cognitive linguistics. He introduced mental spaces as small conceptual packets built during thought and language. These spaces combine dynamically.

Mark Turner, pronounced approximately *mark TER-ner*, born 1954, worked in cognitive science and rhetoric. He co-developed conceptual blending theory and studied how stories, parables, and grammar compress thought. Meaning often comes from integration.

In Idea Theory, blending highlights preservation, suppression, and emergence in real time. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. George Lakoff, pronounced approximately *jorj LAY-kof*, born 1941, worked in cognitive linguistics. He argued that metaphor structures ordinary thought, not merely poetry. Source domains shape target domains.

Charles Fillmore, pronounced approximately *CHARLZ FILL-mor*, 1929 to 2014, worked in linguistics. He founded frame semantics and showed that words evoke structured scenes. To understand buy, sell, price, and cost is to enter a frame.

Daniel Kahneman, pronounced approximately *DAN-yul KAH-nuh-mun*, 1934 to 2024, worked in psychology and behavioral economics. He studied judgment, bias, attention, and two-system thinking. With Amos Tversky he transformed decision science.

David Chalmers, pronounced approximately *DAY-vid CHAL-merz*, born 1966, worked in philosophy of mind. He named the hard problem of consciousness and defended the explanatory gap.

In Idea Theory, the gap becomes a failed translation between first-person and third-person horizons. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Jaegwon Kim, pronounced approximately *JAY-gwun kim*, 1934 to 2019, worked in philosophy of mind. He formulated the causal exclusion problem for mental causation under physicalism.

In Idea Theory, supervenience becomes a question about whether higher-level emergence has causal room. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. ## Social sciences and game theory zoo: thirty-two thinkers

### Kenneth Arrow

Kenneth Arrow, pronounced approximately *KEN-eth AIR-oh*, 1921 to 2017, worked in economics. He proved major results in social choice, general equilibrium, and information economics. His impossibility theorem changed democratic theory.

In Idea Theory, aggregation becomes a problem of gluing local preferences without contradiction. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Friedrich Hayek

Friedrich Hayek, pronounced approximately *FREE-drik HY-ek*, 1899 to 1992, worked in economics and social theory. He emphasized dispersed knowledge and the price system as a discovery procedure. Markets coordinate what no planner knows centrally.

In Idea Theory, prices act like sheaf gluing over local knowledge horizons. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Paul Romer

Paul Romer, pronounced approximately *paul ROH-mer*, born 1955, worked in economics. He made ideas central to endogenous growth theory and stressed non-rival knowledge. Ideas can be used without being used up.

In Idea Theory, non-rival ideas behave like global sections available to many agents. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Martin Weitzman

Martin Weitzman, pronounced approximately *MAR-tin WITES-man*, 1942 to 2019, worked in economics. He modeled recombinant growth, where new ideas arise from combinations of old ones.

In Idea Theory, growth becomes emergence from a widening compositional frontier. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Charles Jones

Charles Jones, pronounced approximately *CHARLZ jonz*, born 1967, worked in economics. He developed semi-endogenous growth models that temper simple idea-driven optimism. Research productivity faces constraints.

In Idea Theory, finite researcher horizons restrict how fast idea combinations can be realized. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

### Joseph Schumpeter

Joseph Schumpeter, pronounced approximately *JOH-sef SHOOM-pay-ter*, 1883 to 1950, worked in economics. He made innovation and creative destruction central to capitalism. Entrepreneurs recombine and disrupt.

In Idea Theory, creative destruction is preservation, suppression, and emergence inside economic life. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. Amos Tversky, pronounced approximately *AH-mohs TVER-skee*, 1937 to 1996, worked in psychology and decision theory. With Daniel Kahneman he discovered framing effects, heuristics, and prospect theory.

In Idea Theory, framing shifts signed valence and meaning curvature in choice. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. The short entry is meant as a handle for re-entering the longer chapter, not as a substitute for the thinker.

Elinor Ostrom, pronounced approximately *EL-ih-nor OSS-trum*, 1933 to 2012, worked in political economy. She showed how communities govern commons without simple privatization or state control.

In Idea Theory, polycentric governance is multi-horizon gluing with local rules. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Listen for the horizon they privilege, the resonance they notice, the kind of stability they seek, and the pressure their life and setting placed on their questions. John von Neumann, pronounced approximately *yon von NOY-man*, 1903 to 1957, worked in mathematics and economics. He helped found game theory, computer architecture, and modern mathematical economics.

In Idea Theory, strategic interaction becomes composition among agents under shared rules. This entry treats the thinker as a guide to one part of the algebra rather than as a slogan. Oskar Morgenstern, pronounced approximately *OSS-kar MOR-gen-shtairn*, 1902 to 1977, worked in economics. With von Neumann he wrote Theory of Games and Economic Behavior and axiomatized expected utility.

## Appendix summary

Appendix A covers one hundred and nineteen entries: fifty-five in philosophy, thirty-two in cognitive science and artificial intelligence, and thirty-two in social science and game theory. Each is a doorway back into the same question: which part of the idea algebra did this thinker make visible?


# Appendix B — Glossary of the Eleven Derived Primitives

Each primitive here is defined three times: one sentence, one paragraph, and one extended walk-through. The point is economy. These are not added knobs. They are ways of reading the horizon category, horizon functor, resonance, grading, composition, and emergence already present.

## Temporality

One sentence: time as a directed region of horizons.

One paragraph: A temporal process is an idea traced along ordered horizons, where earlier and later contexts are connected by disciplined restriction.

Extended walk-through: Imagine hearing a melody. No note alone is the melody, yet the melody is not outside the notes. Temporality names the ordered horizon path that lets earlier content remain available while later content changes what the earlier meant.

Input data: To derive temporality, begin with the ordinary core of Idea Theory rather than with a new faculty. Use horizons, horizon translations, resonance, composition, grading, and the decomposition into preservation, suppression, and emergence. The primitive is legitimate only if those materials suffice.

Construction: The construction asks what has to be selected or traced. Sometimes we select a subregion of horizons. Sometimes we trace a resonance profile. Sometimes we compare degrees across a refinement. Sometimes we look for a fixed point. The important point is that the operation is a reading of existing structure.

No smuggling check: If the explanation secretly assumes time, aboutness, viewpoint, desire, reference, probability, or selfhood before deriving it, it fails. If the explanation can point to the exact horizon structure and the exact resonance or grading behavior doing the work, it passes.

Common mistake: Do not treat temporality as a separate mental box. The whole point of calling it derived is that the familiar word names a pattern already present in the algebra. That pattern may be strong, weak, local, global, stable, or broken. Describing its strength is better than merely announcing that the primitive exists.

## Intentionality

One sentence: aboutness from resonance.

One paragraph: An idea is about something when its resonance profile points coherently toward objects, states, or possibilities across horizons.

Extended walk-through: Think of wanting water. The want is not a picture only, a word only, or a bodily signal only. It is a pattern of directed resonance: cup, thirst, relief, source, action. If the pattern remains coherent across bodily, linguistic, and practical horizons, intentionality has been derived.

Input data: To derive intentionality, begin with the ordinary core of Idea Theory rather than with a new faculty. Use horizons, horizon translations, resonance, composition, grading, and the decomposition into preservation, suppression, and emergence. The primitive is legitimate only if those materials suffice.

Construction: The construction asks what has to be selected or traced. Sometimes we select a subregion of horizons. Sometimes we trace a resonance profile. Sometimes we compare degrees across a refinement. Sometimes we look for a fixed point. The important point is that the operation is a reading of existing structure.

No smuggling check: If the explanation secretly assumes time, aboutness, viewpoint, desire, reference, probability, or selfhood before deriving it, it fails. If the explanation can point to the exact horizon structure and the exact resonance or grading behavior doing the work, it passes.

Common mistake: Do not treat intentionality as a separate mental box. The whole point of calling it derived is that the familiar word names a pattern already present in the algebra. That pattern may be strong, weak, local, global, stable, or broken. Describing its strength is better than merely announcing that the primitive exists.

## Perspective

One sentence: a connected region with a home base.

One paragraph: A perspective is the part of the horizon category an agent can inhabit, together with the horizon from which that agent begins.

Extended walk-through: Your perspective includes your language, body, memories, training, role, and available imaginative moves. It is not a prison, because translations may extend it. It is not nowhere, because every translation begins somewhere.

Input data: To derive perspective, begin with the ordinary core of Idea Theory rather than with a new faculty. Use horizons, horizon translations, resonance, composition, grading, and the decomposition into preservation, suppression, and emergence. The primitive is legitimate only if those materials suffice.

Construction: The construction asks what has to be selected or traced. Sometimes we select a subregion of horizons. Sometimes we trace a resonance profile. Sometimes we compare degrees across a refinement. Sometimes we look for a fixed point. The important point is that the operation is a reading of existing structure.

No smuggling check: If the explanation secretly assumes time, aboutness, viewpoint, desire, reference, probability, or selfhood before deriving it, it fails. If the explanation can point to the exact horizon structure and the exact resonance or grading behavior doing the work, it passes.

Common mistake: Do not treat perspective as a separate mental box. The whole point of calling it derived is that the familiar word names a pattern already present in the algebra. That pattern may be strong, weak, local, global, stable, or broken. Describing its strength is better than merely announcing that the primitive exists.

## Affect or conatus

One sentence: felt striving as change in degree.

One paragraph: Affect is the directional rise or fall in the grading of an idea as a horizon is refined; conatus is the organized tendency to pursue such increases.

Extended walk-through: A project can feel enlivening because, as you imagine future refinements, it gains articulation. Another thought can feel deadening because every refinement lowers its degree. Affect names this directional pressure without adding a new mysterious substance.

Input data: To derive affect or conatus, begin with the ordinary core of Idea Theory rather than with a new faculty. Use horizons, horizon translations, resonance, composition, grading, and the decomposition into preservation, suppression, and emergence. The primitive is legitimate only if those materials suffice.

Construction: The construction asks what has to be selected or traced. Sometimes we select a subregion of horizons. Sometimes we trace a resonance profile. Sometimes we compare degrees across a refinement. Sometimes we look for a fixed point. The important point is that the operation is a reading of existing structure.

Common mistake: Do not treat affect or conatus as a separate mental box. The whole point of calling it derived is that the familiar word names a pattern already present in the algebra. That pattern may be strong, weak, local, global, stable, or broken. Describing its strength is better than merely announcing that the primitive exists.

## Differentiation

One sentence: meaning by resonant contrast.

One paragraph: An idea is differentiated by the alternatives it resonates with but cannot simply combine with.

Extended walk-through: The word tree means partly by not being bush, grass, plank, diagram, or family tree, while still resonating with each. Differentiation is the antidiagonal of meaning: the active field of nearby exclusions.

Input data: To derive differentiation, begin with the ordinary core of Idea Theory rather than with a new faculty. Use horizons, horizon translations, resonance, composition, grading, and the decomposition into preservation, suppression, and emergence. The primitive is legitimate only if those materials suffice.

## Rigid reference

One sentence: naming through stable global support.

One paragraph: A term rigidly refers when it forms a global section whose extension remains singular across the relevant horizons.

Extended walk-through: Aristotle might be described as teacher, biologist, student of Plato, or author. Those descriptions vary. Rigid reference asks whether one name continues to pick out the same individual through those variations.

Input data: To derive rigid reference, begin with the ordinary core of Idea Theory rather than with a new faculty. ## Potentiality

One sentence: what refinement could make actual.

One paragraph: Potentiality is the future resonance an idea can acquire under admissible horizon refinements.

Extended walk-through: A seed, a theorem sketch, a melody fragment, and a young practice all contain more than present actuality. Potentiality is not magic. It is the structured gap between current resonance and resonance available after refinement.

## Schema or paradigm

One sentence: a connected component of intelligibility.

One paragraph: A schema or paradigm is the internally translatable region of horizons within which ideas can move by chains of partial translation.

Extended walk-through: Inside a paradigm, researchers disagree yet understand what counts as evidence, problem, and repair. Outside it, even the questions may fail to translate. Schema names that connected region.

Input data: To derive schema or paradigm, begin with the ordinary core of Idea Theory rather than with a new faculty. Use horizons, horizon translations, resonance, composition, grading, and the decomposition into preservation, suppression, and emergence. The primitive is legitimate only if those materials suffice.
