## Credence

One sentence: probability compatible with horizons.

One paragraph: Credence is a probability assignment at each horizon that remains compatible with the translations between horizons.

Extended walk-through: Belief is not just a number in your head. If you learn, move contexts, or refine evidence, your credences must transform responsibly. Credence is probability living on the horizon functor.

Input data: To derive credence, begin with the ordinary core of Idea Theory rather than with a new faculty. Use horizons, horizon translations, resonance, composition, grading, and the decomposition into preservation, suppression, and emergence. The primitive is legitimate only if those materials suffice.

Construction: The construction asks what has to be selected or traced. Sometimes we select a subregion of horizons. Sometimes we trace a resonance profile. Sometimes we compare degrees across a refinement. Sometimes we look for a fixed point. The important point is that the operation is a reading of existing structure.

No smuggling check: If the explanation secretly assumes time, aboutness, viewpoint, desire, reference, probability, or selfhood before deriving it, it fails. If the explanation can point to the exact horizon structure and the exact resonance or grading behavior doing the work, it passes.

Common mistake: Do not treat credence as a separate mental box. The whole point of calling it derived is that the familiar word names a pattern already present in the algebra. That pattern may be strong, weak, local, global, stable, or broken. Describing its strength is better than merely announcing that the primitive exists.

## Signed valence

One sentence: approach and avoidance from self-resonance.

One paragraph: Signed valence is the attraction or aversion that appears when an agent self-representation composes with an idea.

Extended walk-through: A proposal may draw you in because it preserves your self-understanding, or repel you because it threatens it. Signed valence derives that felt plus or minus from resonance and composition.

Input data: To derive signed valence, begin with the ordinary core of Idea Theory rather than with a new faculty. Use horizons, horizon translations, resonance, composition, grading, and the decomposition into preservation, suppression, and emergence. The primitive is legitimate only if those materials suffice.

Construction: The construction asks what has to be selected or traced. Sometimes we select a subregion of horizons. Sometimes we trace a resonance profile. Sometimes we compare degrees across a refinement. Sometimes we look for a fixed point. The important point is that the operation is a reading of existing structure.

No smuggling check: If the explanation secretly assumes time, aboutness, viewpoint, desire, reference, probability, or selfhood before deriving it, it fails. If the explanation can point to the exact horizon structure and the exact resonance or grading behavior doing the work, it passes.

Common mistake: Do not treat signed valence as a separate mental box. The whole point of calling it derived is that the familiar word names a pattern already present in the algebra. That pattern may be strong, weak, local, global, stable, or broken. Describing its strength is better than merely announcing that the primitive exists.

## Reflexion

One sentence: self-application of the structure.

One paragraph: Reflexion occurs when the idea algebra applies to itself and reaches a stable self-referential pattern.

Extended walk-through: A person thinking about thinking, a proof system representing its own provability, or a tradition criticizing its own conditions all display reflexion. The theory turns back on itself and asks whether the turn stabilizes.

Input data: To derive reflexion, begin with the ordinary core of Idea Theory rather than with a new faculty. Use horizons, horizon translations, resonance, composition, grading, and the decomposition into preservation, suppression, and emergence. The primitive is legitimate only if those materials suffice.

Construction: The construction asks what has to be selected or traced. Sometimes we select a subregion of horizons. Sometimes we trace a resonance profile. Sometimes we compare degrees across a refinement. Sometimes we look for a fixed point. The important point is that the operation is a reading of existing structure.

No smuggling check: If the explanation secretly assumes time, aboutness, viewpoint, desire, reference, probability, or selfhood before deriving it, it fails. If the explanation can point to the exact horizon structure and the exact resonance or grading behavior doing the work, it passes.

Common mistake: Do not treat reflexion as a separate mental box. The whole point of calling it derived is that the familiar word names a pattern already present in the algebra. That pattern may be strong, weak, local, global, stable, or broken. Describing its strength is better than merely announcing that the primitive exists.

## Appendix summary

The eleven derived primitives show the main methodological promise of the book. Time, aboutness, viewpoint, feeling, contrast, reference, possibility, paradigm, belief, attraction, and self-reference do not need to be pasted onto the theory from outside. They can be reconstructed from the structure already doing the work.


# Appendix C — Glossary of the Fifteen Theorems

Each theorem is restated in three voices: headline, paragraph form, and careful semi-formal restatement. The restatements use role names rather than symbols so they can be heard clearly.

## the composition theorem: Composition

Headline: *Ideas can be composed without ambiguity, and the silent idea is unique.*

Paragraph form: This rules out systems where putting ideas together depends on arbitrary grouping. It predicts that stable intellectual practices will have repeatable chains, neutral steps, and sometimes reversible operations.

Careful semi-formal restatement: Within each horizon, the carrier of ideas has a composition operation and an identity idea. Composition is associative, the identity leaves every idea unchanged on either side, and the ideas that can be reversed under composition form a coherent reversible subsystem.

Listening example: When you hear the composition theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the composition theorem: Composition with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the resonance theorem: Resonance pairing

Headline: *Once basic resonances are fixed, resonance extends consistently to larger constructions.*

Paragraph form: This rules out arbitrary after-the-fact similarity scores. It predicts that phrase meanings, prototype effects, and semantic fit will be constrained by the resonance of their parts.

Careful semi-formal restatement: The resonance pairing assigns directional fit between ideas and extends to combinations in the unique way compatible with addition of content and scaling of weight, while preserving non-degeneracy where the structure requires it.

Listening example: When you hear the resonance theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the resonance theorem: Resonance pairing with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the aufhebung theorem: Aufhebung decomposition

Headline: *Every meaningful composition preserves something, suppresses something, and generates something new.*

Paragraph form: This rules out the idea that combination is mere addition. It predicts that metaphors, revolutions, compromises, and institutions will display residue, cancellation, and novelty at once.

Careful semi-formal restatement: For every pair of ideas in a horizon, their composition decomposes into a preservation part, a suppression part, and an emergence part, with uniqueness measured by the resonance structure and with no extra component smuggled in.

Listening example: When you hear the aufhebung theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the aufhebung theorem: Aufhebung decomposition with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the emergence cocycle theorem: Emergence cocycle

Headline: *Novelty is coherent across longer chains of composition.*

Paragraph form: This rules out mystical emergence that can say anything at any time. It predicts that cumulative novelty can be tracked through teaching, tradition, technology, and scientific change.

Careful semi-formal restatement: The emergence part satisfies a closure condition across triples of ideas, is normalized at the identity idea, and determines a stable obstruction class measuring irreducible novelty.

Listening example: When you hear the emergence cocycle theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the emergence cocycle theorem: Emergence cocycle with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the meaning curvature theorem: Meaning curvature

Headline: *Directional asymmetry in resonance leaves a durable invariant.*

Paragraph form: This rules out the fantasy that all framing effects are removable wording errors. It predicts persistent asymmetries in metaphor, salience, order, and political framing.

Careful semi-formal restatement: The antisymmetric component of the resonance pairing, called meaning curvature, is closed under the natural comparison of contexts and determines a structural invariant preserved by equivalence of idea algebras.

Listening example: When you hear the meaning curvature theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the meaning curvature theorem: Meaning curvature with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the conjugation theorem: Conjugation and frames

Headline: *Reframing can preserve internal structure while changing external resonance.*

Paragraph form: This rules out both naive relativism and naive neutrality. It predicts that two agents can agree inside a frame and still disagree about what the frame means from outside.

Careful semi-formal restatement: Conjugation by an invertible frame sends ideas to reframed ideas, preserves composition, identity, decomposition, emergence, and grading internally, while allowing the resonance profile against outside ideas to be reweighted.

Listening example: When you hear the conjugation theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the conjugation theorem: Conjugation and frames with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the transmission theorem: Transmission chains

Headline: *Long transmission can be analyzed step by step without losing the total effect.*

Paragraph form: This rules out treating diffusion as an unanalyzable blur. It predicts that cultural and pedagogical chains contain removable silent steps and accumulated emergence.

Careful semi-formal restatement: A transmission chain is an iterated composition in which outputs feed later inputs, identity steps may be inserted or removed without changing the transmitted content, and the total transformation is recovered from the ordered chain.

Listening example: When you hear the transmission theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the transmission theorem: Transmission chains with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the equivalence theorem: Structural equivalence

Headline: *Different expressions can carry the same idea when their structure evaluates the same way.*

Paragraph form: This rules out confusing syntax with meaning. It predicts synonymy, translation, and model equivalence when composition, resonance, and decomposition are preserved.

Careful semi-formal restatement: Two idea expressions are structurally equivalent when a structure-preserving translation carries one to the other while preserving composition, identity, resonance, decomposition, emergence, grading, and horizon behavior.

Listening example: When you hear the equivalence theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the equivalence theorem: Structural equivalence with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the graded structure theorem: Graded idea algebra

Headline: *Ideas can have degrees that behave consistently under composition.*

Paragraph form: This rules out treating depth as mere taste. It predicts hierarchy, abstraction, proof length, conceptual complexity, and learning level as structured features.

Careful semi-formal restatement: Each idea receives a degree from an ordered grading system, the degree of a composition is determined by the degrees of the composed ideas, and horizon translations respect grading where the relevant preservation conditions hold.

Listening example: When you hear the graded structure theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the graded structure theorem: Graded idea algebra with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the coalitions theorem: Coalitions

Headline: *Groups of agents or sub-ideas can aggregate when cohesion conditions hold.*

Paragraph form: This rules out assuming that groups are either mere sums or mysterious wholes. It predicts committees, coalitions, and institutions that preserve detectable internal alignment.

Careful semi-formal restatement: A coalition aggregates member ideas or agents through a social composition operation, has identity and silent-member behavior, and satisfies congruence conditions that make the aggregate stable under recognized translations.

Listening example: When you hear the coalitions theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the coalitions theorem: Coalitions with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the diffusion theorem: Cultural transmission

Headline: *Transmission itself forms an algebra with inheritance and faithful propagation.*

Paragraph form: This rules out one-off copying models of culture. It predicts lineages, inherited structure, and stable propagation across generations when horizon translations preserve enough content.

Careful semi-formal restatement: Cultural transmission is modeled as compositional propagation through agents and horizons, with identity cases, faithful maps, and inherited structure that can be compared across chains.

Listening example: When you hear the diffusion theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the diffusion theorem: Cultural transmission with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the novelty theorem: Emergent novelty

Headline: *Some outputs are genuinely outside their prior closure.*

Paragraph form: This rules out calling every rearrangement creative. It predicts identifiable innovations when a composition cannot be recovered from the earlier lineage alone.

Careful semi-formal restatement: An idea is emergently novel when its composed output is not contained in the closure generated by its predecessors under the allowed operations, while still being produced by a lawful act of composition.

Listening example: When you hear the novelty theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the novelty theorem: Emergent novelty with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the hermeneutic sheaf theorem: Hermeneutic sheaf

Headline: *Understanding is local agreement that glues into a unique whole.*

Paragraph form: This rules out purely private understanding and also rules out mere public repetition. It predicts successful interpretation when local readings agree on overlaps.

Careful semi-formal restatement: Given a covering family of sub-horizons, local idea data count as understood at the larger horizon exactly when the restrictions agree on all overlaps and glue uniquely to a global section.

Listening example: When you hear the hermeneutic sheaf theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the hermeneutic sheaf theorem: Hermeneutic sheaf with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the indexical pullback theorem: Indexical pullback

Headline: *Inference and resonance need not survive a change of horizon.*

Paragraph form: This rules out context-free semantics for indexicals, paradigms, and frames. It predicts translation failures that are repairable in some cases and irreducible in others.

Careful semi-formal restatement: A horizon translation preserves composition and identity, but preservation part, emergence part, resonance, and grading may fail to pull back strictly; the obstruction measures the failure of universal inference across horizons.

Listening example: When you hear the indexical pullback theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the indexical pullback theorem: Indexical pullback with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## the profundity theorem: Profundity

Headline: *Depth is broad horizon support plus faithful emergence-preserving transmission.*

Paragraph form: This rules out treating profundity as mere intensity or prestige. It predicts that the deepest ideas travel widely without becoming empty and generate further structure without losing themselves.

Careful semi-formal restatement: The grading of a profound idea factors through the breadth of horizons on which it has coherent support and through its closure under emergence, so maximal profundity belongs to ideas stable across translations and faithful under transmission.

Listening example: When you hear the profundity theorem, imagine an ordinary case before imagining a formal one. A classroom explanation, a legal rule, a metaphor, a market signal, or a scientific model either keeps structure stable or fails in a describable way. The theorem tells you what kind of stability or failure to listen for.

Diagnostic question: Ask what would have to be observed for this theorem to matter in practice. Does a composition stay coherent? Does a frame change resonance? Does a translation preserve inference? Does a group aggregate without losing cohesion? Does a novelty really exceed its lineage? The theorem is not a label for admiration; it is a test for structure.

Memory cue: Pair the profundity theorem: Profundity with one ordinary verb. Compose, fit, split, cohere, curve, reframe, transmit, translate, grade, aggregate, inherit, innovate, glue, shift, or deepen. The verb is not the theorem, but it gives the ear a doorway back into the careful statement.

## Appendix summary

Together the fifteen theorems say that ideas are compositional, resonant, decomposable, novel in a disciplined way, curved by framing, reframable, transmissible, structurally comparable, graded, socially aggregable, culturally inheritable, sometimes genuinely new, locally gluable, context-sensitive, and profound when they remain stable across horizons.


# Appendix D — A Pronunciation Guide

This appendix is for the ear. It gives approximate English pronunciations and reminds you of each term's role in the book. The glosses are guides, not claims about perfect native pronunciation.

## Greek letter role names

The book avoids reading Greek letters aloud in the chapters, but the website uses them. The horizon functor is pronounced *KSIGH* or *ksee* if you say the letter, but in the audiobook it means the rule assigning a full idea algebra to each horizon. The resonance pairing is the directional fit between ideas. The preservation part, suppression part, and emergence part are the three flavors of composition. The emergence cocycle is the coherence law for novelty across chains. Meaning curvature is the asymmetric residue of resonance. Degree or grading is the ordered depth or complexity of an idea. The horizon category is the organized field of contexts and translations.

For derived primitives, the website may use Greek letters, but the audiobook uses role names. Temporality is time as ordered horizons. Intentionality is aboutness. Perspective is an agent's connected region. Affect or conatus is striving. Differentiation is meaning by contrast. Rigid reference is stable naming. Potentiality is future resonance. Schema or paradigm is connected intelligibility. Credence is probability over horizons. Signed valence is attraction or aversion. Reflexion is self-application.

## German terms

Aufhebung is pronounced approximately *OWF-hay-boong*. It means cancellation, preservation, and lifting-up at once, and the book treats it as the threefold decomposition of composition.

Dasein is pronounced approximately *DAH-zine*. In Heidegger it means the being for whom Being is an issue. In this book it is heard as a perspective with a home horizon.

Sorge is pronounced approximately *ZOR-guh*. It means care. It names the directed concern by which a life is oriented toward possibilities.

Begriffsgeschichte is pronounced approximately *buh-GRIFFS-guh-shikh-tuh*. It means conceptual history. Koselleck uses it to track concepts across historical horizons.

Weltanschauung is pronounced approximately *VELT-ahn-show-oong*. It means worldview. In this book it is close to a large schema or paradigm.

Zeitgeist is pronounced approximately *TSITE-gyste*. It means spirit of the age. It names the atmospheric horizon of a historical moment.

## French terms

Differance is pronounced approximately *dee-fay-RAHNS*. Derrida's spelling marks both difference and deferral. In this book it is linked to differentiation, trace, and temporal suppression.

Habitus is pronounced approximately *HAH-bee-toos* in a French setting, though English speakers often say *HAB-ih-tus*. For Bourdieu, it is embodied social disposition. Here it is a durable perspective shaped by field and practice.

Episteme is pronounced approximately *eh-pee-STEM-ay*. For Foucault, it is the historical condition of what can count as knowledge. Here it is a paradigm component of the horizon category.

Ecriture is pronounced approximately *ay-kree-TOOR*. It means writing. In post-structuralist settings it reminds us that meaning leaves traces beyond immediate speech.

Ressentiment is pronounced approximately *ruh-sahn-tee-MAHN*. Nietzsche uses it for reactive moral formation. In Idea Theory it is a patterned valence and suppression structure.

## Sanskrit and Pali terms

Shunyata is pronounced approximately *shoon-yah-TAH*. It is usually translated as emptiness. In the book it marks the absence of independent self-support and becomes a reflexive insight.

Pratityasamutpada is pronounced approximately *prah-TEE-tyah-sah-moot-PAH-dah*. It means dependent origination. It says that things arise through conditions rather than from isolated essence.

Dharma is pronounced approximately *DAR-muh*. Depending on context it can mean teaching, law, phenomenon, or truth. The horizon matters.

Anatman is pronounced approximately *ah-NAHT-mun*. It means no fixed self. In this book it resonates with the critique of a privileged home horizon.

Dukkha is pronounced approximately *DOOK-kah*. It means suffering, unsatisfactoriness, or stress. It is useful when speaking of affective horizons.

## Classical Chinese names and terms

Zhuangzi is pronounced approximately *jwahng-DZUH*. He is the Daoist master of shifting perspectives, dreams, and playful reversals.

Confucius is pronounced approximately *kun-FYOO-shus*. His Chinese name Kongzi is pronounced approximately *KONG-dzuh*. He teaches ritual, humaneness, learning, and rectified names.

Ren is pronounced approximately *run* with a soft beginning. It means humaneness or authoritative benevolence. In this book it is a moral relation stabilized by practice.

Li is pronounced approximately *lee*. It means ritual propriety, patterned conduct, and the forms through which humaneness becomes social.

Dao is pronounced approximately *dow*. It means way or path. It names a horizon that cannot be exhausted by explicit rules.

Wu wei is pronounced approximately *woo way*. It means non-forcing or effortless action, not mere passivity.

## Names often heard in clusters

Husserl is *HUSS-url*, Heidegger is *HY-deg-er*, Gadamer is *GAH-duh-mer*, and Ricoeur is *rih-KUR*. Hear them as the phenomenology and hermeneutics cluster: intentionality, perspective, care, and fusion of horizons.

Saussure is *soh-SOOR*, Derrida is *deh-ree-DAH*, Foucault is *foo-KOH*, and Deleuze is *duh-LOOZ*. Hear them as the structural and post-structural cluster: differentiation, trace, episteme, and mobile concepts.

Peirce is *purse*, James is *jaymz*, and Dewey is *DOO-ee*. Hear them as the pragmatist cluster: sign, consequence, inquiry, and repair. Quine is *kwine*, Kripke is *KRIP-kee*, and Brandom is *BRAN-dum*. Hear them as the analytic cluster: translation, reference, and inference.

For Sanskrit and Chinese terms, do not rush. A slow approximate pronunciation is better than a fast confident blur. These words carry whole traditions, and the audiobook uses them as philosophical partners rather than exotic ornaments.

## Stress patterns for repeated listening

When a name has three or more syllables, choose one strong stress and keep moving. Merleau-Ponty carries its stress near the end. Kierkegaard carries a strong opening and a softened ending. Maimonides has the weight in the middle. Koselleck begins firmly and closes quickly. Pratityasamutpada should be slowed into several pieces rather than forced into one rush.

For terms with accents, the audiobook favors clarity over typographic display. Episteme, differance, and shunyata are explained by role as soon as they appear. If the listener remembers the role, the pronunciation has done its work.

## How to pronounce without losing meaning

The safest pattern is name, gloss, role. Say the name approximately, give the English cue, and then give the Idea Theory role. For example: Aufhebung, *OWF-hay-boong*, the threefold pattern of preserving, suppressing, and lifting into emergence. Dasein, *DAH-zine*, the being whose own horizon matters to it. Differance, *dee-fay-RAHNS*, the delay and difference that prevent meaning from becoming pure presence.

The same method works for names. Nagarjuna, *nah-GAR-joo-nah*, the philosopher of emptiness and dependent origination. Zhuangzi, *jwahng-DZUH*, the philosopher of perspective shifts and playful reversal. Saussure, *soh-SOOR*, the theorist of differential value. Hayek, *HY-ek*, the theorist of dispersed knowledge. A pronunciation guide is therefore also a conceptual index.

Some listeners will know more accurate pronunciations. That is welcome. The aim here is not to replace living languages with simplified English. The aim is to prevent the audio from turning unfamiliar words into static. A clear approximation keeps the idea available for thought.

## Appendix summary

Pronunciation is part of hospitality. The goal is not accent perfection but respectful audibility: say the term clearly, remember its role, and let the idea rejoin the argument.


# Appendix E — Suggested Listening Routes

The book can be heard straight through, but it was also built for routes. Part Six, the Lean formalization, is intentionally omitted from this audiobook. For those interested in the formal Lean verification, see the project website. The audiobook gives the concepts, the theorem stories, the zoos, and the closing synthesis, but it does not narrate the machine-checked proofs.

## The philosophy major's path

Start with the welcome and the one-sitting overview. Then listen to the formal core through the horizon functor and derived primitives. You do not need every theorem proof on first pass, but you should hear the theorem review. Then move to the philosophy zoo: antiquity, medieval thought, early modern philosophy, German idealism, phenomenology, structuralism, pragmatism, analytic philosophy, and non-Western traditions. End with Part Seven and Appendices B, C, and D.

This route asks: which old disputes were about different horizons, and which remain genuinely unresolved?

## The cognitive scientist's path

Begin with the overview, then hear the chapters on composition, resonance, decomposition, emergence, grading, meaning curvature, horizons, and derived primitives. After that, go to the cognitive science and artificial intelligence zoo. Pay special attention to prototype theory, situated conceptualization, blending, conceptual metaphor, frame semantics, distributional semantics, transformers, cultural evolution, emergence, and the philosophy of mind chapters.

This route asks: what empirical signatures should appear if concepts are horizon-relative resonance structures?

## The economist or game theorist's path

Begin with the overview and the formal core in summary. Make sure to hear transmission chains, structural equivalence, grading, the hermeneutic sheaf theorem, the indexical pullback theorem, and the profundity theorem. Then move to the social-science and game-theory zoo: Arrow, Hayek, Romer, Weitzman, Schumpeter, Kahneman and Tversky, Ostrom, von Neumann and Morgenstern, Nash, Schelling, Aumann, Selten, Bourdieu, Latour, and the Wittgenstein-Nash bridge.

This route asks: when do local signals, preferences, and conventions glue into a stable social order?

## The Lean-curious path

Because Part Six is intentionally not included in this audiobook, the Lean-curious listener should use the website as the technical companion. Hear Parts Zero, One, and Two in the audiobook, then visit the project website for the formal Lean verification, theorem files, and proof pages. Return to Appendix C afterward, because the plain-language theorem glossary will make more sense once you have seen how the proofs are organized.

This route asks: how can informal philosophical claims be made auditable without losing their human meaning?

## The complete bath

The complete bath is every chapter in order: orientation, formal core, theorem stories, philosophy zoo, cognitive science and artificial intelligence zoo, social-science and game-theory zoo, Part Seven, and all appendices. It is meant as roughly forty hours of listening, depending on narration speed. Do it while walking, commuting, cooking, or recovering from other forms of noise.

This route asks the largest question: can one compact formal grammar help you hear the history of ideas, the science of concepts, and the social life of knowledge as one continuous conversation?

## A first-week plan

If you want a practical seven-day route, hear the welcome and overview on day one. Hear composition, resonance, and the threefold decomposition on day two. Hear emergence, grading, curvature, and horizons on day three. Hear the derived primitives on day four. Hear the theorem review on day five. Sample one philosophy chapter, one cognitive science chapter, and one social-science chapter on day six. Hear Part Seven on day seven. After that, choose your specialized route.

## A re-listening route

On a second pass, do not try to hear everything. Choose one motif: resonance, horizon, emergence, or translation. Follow only that motif through the chapters. This turns the book from an encyclopedia into a set of variations. The same chapter will sound different when the guiding question changes. That is not a problem. It is the method of horizons applied to listening itself.

## A route for teachers and reading groups

For a seminar, assign the overview, one formal-core chapter, one theorem chapter, and two zoo chapters each week. Ask participants to bring one example from outside the book and translate it into the vocabulary: composition, resonance, horizon, preservation, suppression, emergence, and curvature. The discussion will quickly reveal which parts of the framework have become usable and which remain only memorized.

For a reading group, rotate roles. One listener tracks examples. One tracks definitions. One tracks objections. One tracks empirical predictions. This makes the group itself a small horizon category, with each participant supplying a local reading that may or may not glue.

## A route for skeptics

If you are skeptical of grand theories, do not begin with the whole system. Begin with the limits chapter, then hear the disputes chapter, then hear the empirical predictions chapter. After that, choose one theorem and one zoo chapter where you already know the material. Ask whether the framework clarifies anything you did not already have words for. If it does not, keep the objection. If it does, move outward slowly.

The skeptical route is important because the book should not win by atmosphere. It should win, where it wins, by making distinctions easier to use.

## Appendix summary

Choose the route that matches your purpose. The whole book is available, but the best first route is the one that gives you enough structure to start using the vocabulary immediately.


# Appendix F — Further Reading and Project Website Pointers

The audiobook is the listening layer. The project website is the reading, browsing, and verification layer. Use it when you want theorem pages, formal definitions, Lean files, chapter bridges, zoo entries, and cross-links that would be tedious to hear aloud.

## Formal core pages

For composition, identity, resonance, the threefold decomposition, emergence, grading, meaning curvature, structural equivalence, transmission chains, the horizon category, and the horizon functor, begin with the website's formalism section. Those pages give the exact role names, the symbolic versions for readers who want them, and worked examples that correspond to the early formal-core chapters.

For derived primitives, go especially to the horizon formalism page. It gives the source construction for temporality, intentionality, perspective, affect, differentiation, rigid reference, potentiality, schema, credence, signed valence, and reflexion. The audiobook restates these in plain speech; the website shows how they sit inside the full structure.

## Theorem pages

For the fifteen theorems, use the website theorem index. It gives the plain-English map, links to individual theorem pages, and summaries of the supporting Lean lemmas. The most important pages for review are composition, resonance pairing, Aufhebung decomposition, emergence cocycle, meaning curvature, conjugation, transmission chain, structural equivalence, graded idea algebra, coalitions, cultural transmission, novelty, and the three horizon-functor theorems.

For those interested in the formal Lean verification, follow the website's Lean section. This audiobook intentionally omits the proof narration, but the project website contains the technical route.

## Zoo pages

For Part Three, use the philosophy zoo pages as companions to the thinker chapters. They help you compare Plato on Forms, Aristotle on potentiality, Hegel on sublation, Husserl on intentionality, Heidegger on care, Gadamer on fusion, Saussure on differentiation, Derrida on differance, Foucault on episteme, Wittgenstein on language games, Quine on indeterminacy, Kripke on rigid designation, Nagarjuna on emptiness, Confucius on ritual, and Zhuangzi on perspective.

For Part Four, use the cognitive science and artificial intelligence zoo. Look for prototype theory, theory-theory, situated conceptualization, blending, conceptual metaphor, frames, conceptual spaces, heuristics and biases, tensor-product representations, holographic representations, word embeddings, transformers, deep learning, large language models, memetics, epidemiology of representations, cumulative culture, emergence, and consciousness.

For Part Five, use the social science and game theory zoo. The relevant entries include information as a commodity, dispersed knowledge, endogenous growth, recombinant growth, creative destruction, framing, commons governance, expected utility, equilibrium, focal points, evolutionarily stable strategies, cooperation, common knowledge, equilibrium refinement, unit ideas, weak ties, habitus, actor networks, contextualism, conceptual history, standpoint, scientific norms, speech acts, convention, radical interpretation, implicature, paradigms, and the social self.

## Classical sources

For primary texts, begin with Plato's Republic, Aristotle's Metaphysics and De Anima, Augustine's Confessions, Descartes's Meditations, Spinoza's Ethics, Hume's Treatise, Kant's Critique of Pure Reason, Hegel's Phenomenology of Spirit, Nietzsche's Genealogy of Morals, Husserl's Ideas, Heidegger's Being and Time, Gadamer's Truth and Method, Wittgenstein's Philosophical Investigations, Quine's Word and Object, Kripke's Naming and Necessity, Nagarjuna's Fundamental Verses, the Analects, and the Zhuangzi.

For scientific and social companions, read Rosch on prototypes, Barsalou on situated concepts, Fauconnier and Turner on blending, Lakoff and Johnson on metaphor, Fillmore on frames, Kahneman and Tversky on judgment, Dawkins on memes, Sperber on cultural epidemiology, Henrich on cumulative culture, Hayek on dispersed knowledge, Romer and Weitzman on ideas and growth, Ostrom on commons governance, Nash and Aumann on game theory, Bourdieu on habitus, Latour on actor networks, Skinner and Koselleck on historical meaning, and Kuhn on scientific revolutions.

## How to move between audiobook and website

A good rhythm is to listen first, browse second, and return third. Listen to a chapter without stopping. Then visit the corresponding website page and look at the definitions, theorem links, and zoo connections. Finally, replay the chapter or its summary. The second hearing is usually clearer because the website has supplied visual anchors that the ear did not have.

For readers with formal training, the theorem pages are the bridge. For readers with historical interests, the zoo pages are the bridge. For readers with programming interests, the Lean pages are the bridge. For teachers, the appendices are the bridge, because they gather pronunciations, theorem statements, primitives, and routes in a form easy to assign.

## A caution about further reading

Do not try to read every primary source at once. The better path is triangular. Pick one formal idea, one historical thinker, and one empirical or social-scientific application. For example, pair meaning curvature with Nietzsche and Kahneman. Pair the hermeneutic sheaf theorem with Gadamer and Hayek. Pair rigid reference with Kripke and word embeddings. Pair emergence with Hegel, Broad, and recombinant growth.

This triangular method keeps the theory honest. If the formal idea cannot illuminate both a historical text and a contemporary case, it may be too vague. If it illuminates both while preserving their differences, it is doing real work.

## Suggested pairings

For Plato, pair the Republic with the website material on rigid reference, grading, and profundity. For Aristotle, pair the Metaphysics with potentiality and emergence. For Hegel, pair the Logic or Phenomenology with the threefold decomposition and emergence cocycle. For Saussure, pair the Course in General Linguistics with differentiation. For Wittgenstein, pair the Investigations with horizons and language games.

For empirical work, pair Rosch with resonance, Barsalou with perspective, Fauconnier and Turner with emergence, Kahneman and Tversky with curvature, Henrich with transmission, Hayek with sheaf gluing, Nash with fixed points, and Aumann with common knowledge. Each pairing is a way of testing whether the formal vocabulary illuminates a real literature rather than merely renaming it.

## Appendix summary

Use the website when you want exact structure, links, theorem details, or Lean verification. Use the audiobook when you want the argument to become audible as one continuous line of thought.
